question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 13470/22-27, why his Department has not published the outcome of the 2021 consultation.
answer_text: As stated in my answer to AQW 13470/22-27, a comprehensive report of the consultation, indicating how responses were considered in the formulation of policy, will be published following a decision by the Executive on the way forward. It is frustrating that the future of RHI is still undecided. It is a problem I inherited, and a problem that should have been dealt with long ago.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 559/22-27, why the data relating to abortions in Northern Ireland, published on 20 June 2024, does not contain the same information as that published in England and Wales.
answer_text: In response to AQO 559/22-27, I explained that the then forthcoming publication for abortions in Northern Ireland would provide a breakdown of abortions by age, by Health & Social Care Trust, by the statutory grounds for abortion, by gestation and by method of abortion.  The statistics published on 20 June 2024 provides this breakdown, however, the number of surgical abortions were not reported on separately given the small numbers and to prevent patient disclosure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the High Court judgement in the case brought by Dennison Commercials against his Department, whether he will lift restrictions on other  Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme members and refund monies due.
answer_text: This is an important judgment and the Department intends to take the time required to reflect on the detailed points therein.  The RHI scheme is clearly not fit for purpose. That is why New Decade, New Approach committed to closing RHI and replacing it with more effective support. This is a problem I have inherited and have for some time been attempting to secure agreement at the Executive on delivering the NDNA commitment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the consultation that has taken place with affected stakeholders in relation to the suggested closure of the Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: My Department previously carried out a public consultation in 2021 on the future of the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. The consultation document is available on the Department’s website (Northern Ireland Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme – Future of the Scheme) A comprehensive report of the consultation, indicating how responses were considered in the formulation of policy, will be published following a decision by the Executive on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the level of compensation being proposed in relation to the suggested closure of the Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: The Executive has not yet reached a decision in relation to the closure of the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. I aim to ensure that the closure payment mechanism will be fair to both the participants, who joined the Scheme in good faith, and the taxpayer who funds the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the intended timeline for the suggested closure of the Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: I am working to secure the Executive’s agreement to close the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme.  Delivery and implementation is likely to take at least 12 to 18 months but this timeline will be subject to various milestones that may extend this, such as delays in Executive approval or delays in agreement of legislation.  I hope to deliver a permanent solution as soon as possible to ensure fairness to those who joined the Scheme in good faith, and the taxpayer who funds the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the anticipated cost of the suggested closure of the Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.
answer_text: The Executive has not yet reached a decision about the closure of the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. My officials are continuing to engage with the Department of Finance, HMT and legal advisors on this matter to determine an appropriate closure payment mechanism that is affordable and ensures that there is fairness to both the participants, who joined the Scheme in good faith, and the taxpayer who funds the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given there is a bluetongue vaccine, to detail why it is not being used to address issues in relation to livestock movements from Great Britain.
answer_text: There are currently no UK or EU authorised vaccines for Bluetongue serotype 3 (BTV-3). Recently, the Netherlands has authorised the use of a BTV-3 vaccine via emergency authorisation procedure for use in its own jurisdiction only. While Defra retain the ability to also do this no vaccine has been authorised for use in the UK at the present time. The UK Government is, however, actively engaging with the pharmaceutical industry on vaccine development and my officials are regularly updated on progress.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether the announcement made by the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on 15 September 2021, that checks under the Pet Travel Regulations on all pet dogs, cats and ferrets travelling from Great Britain to Northern Ireland would be suspended indefinitely, is still operative; and (ii) to detail the current situation in relation to these checks.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, Pet Travel from Great Britain to Northern Ireland is now a matter that no longer sits within my ministerial responsibilities and is now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline why data is not published in relation to abortions in Northern Ireland, similar to the statistics published for England and Wales.
answer_text: The Department will be publishing comprehensive data relating to abortions in Northern Ireland on 20 June 2024.  This will cover the three year period from the introduction of the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No 2) Regulations on 31 March 2020 until 31 March 2023.My understanding is that the Department’s annual publications had under-reported the number of abortions in Northern Ireland since the law was changed in 2020 - this is recognised and, although the accurate totals had been disclosed regularly through written Assembly Questions, I can assure the Member that this will be corrected in the forthcoming publication for each of the three reporting years. The issue arose because the methodology in place prior to the law change was set up to capture only those procedures involving hospital admissions, however since the introduction of the 2020 Abortion Regulations, the majority of abortions in Northern Ireland are early medical abortions and take place in an outpatient setting.  Following the instruction from the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland to the Department on 2 December 2022 to formally commission abortion services, a process has been under way to verify up-to-date statistics from the date of law change in Northern Ireland – these are the statistics that will be published later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why uninhabitability and impecuniosity are of no relevance when it comes to the service and upholding of a completion notice under Article 25B and Schedule 8B of the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977, but are relevant to the decisions in relation to the Victoria Square complex.
answer_text: The considerations for the service of a completion notice, and the recent decisions in relation to the Victoria Square complex are not comparable. 		Completion notices for a new dwelling set a “completion date” within a three-month period for when the building could reasonably be completed and included in the Valuation List for the first time. Under that mechanism the normal valuation assessment provisions are set aside, between the completion date and the day on which the building is deemed capable of rateable occupation (“the completion period”). The legislation, to prevent long term avoidance of the vacant rating charge, expressly permits the Department to assume that the property is complete, ready for occupation and in a reasonable state of repair for the purpose for which it was intended. 		A 12-month developer’s exclusion, applicable to both commercial home development and “self -builds”, can then apply from the completion date while the property remains vacant. 		The decisions pertaining to the Victoria Square complex were made in respect of properties which were both already in the valuation list and which had previously been in rateable occupation. This is outside the completion period mechanism noted above, and thus the standard provisions for assessing capital value in the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977, supplemented by valuation case law, applied.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the cost of the loan of portraits currently on display in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The items currently on display in Parliament Buildings were agreed by the Assembly Commission following a comprehensive review to agree a collection of items which was inclusive and reflective of the range of political opinion within the parliamentary history of Parliament Buildings.  The collection was installed in March 2022.  The collection includes both original and reproduction portraits but there are no items on loan to the Assembly Commission and no ongoing costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how bodies of aborted babies are dealt with in NHS facilities.
answer_text: Arrangements are in place within Health and Social Care Trusts for the sensitive management of pregnancy remains.The management of pregnancy remains both in the context of miscarriage and abortion is the same and is discussed with the woman at the time the woman is receiving care.There is variation across Trusts in terms of the burial or cremation of pregnancy remains.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the regulations that have been made under Section 27(5) of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 pertaining to the proposal of application notices as to who must be notified; and (ii) , in the absence of such regulations, what are the requirements as to who must be notified.
answer_text: My Department has not made regulations under the discretionary powers afforded by section 27(5) of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (the Act) relating to the requirement to give proposal of application notices to specified persons.  Section 27(2) of the Act requires that a prospective applicant must give notice to the appropriate council where the application for planning permission for the development is to be submitted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of withdrawals from Relationships and Sexuality Education classes in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) is a compulsory element of the curriculum.As you are aware, the Relationships and Sexuality Education (Northern Ireland) (Amendment) Regulations 2023 amended the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 and the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 in respect of Learning for Life and Work (LLW) to ensure that pupils at key stages 3 and 4 have the opportunity to “Receive age appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights, covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion”.   So whilst RSE is compulsory, parents have the right to request that their child be withdrawn from these elements of RSE only and Circular 2024/1 issued by my Department in January 2024 provides guidance to schools who have the responsibility of managing this. My Department does not currently hold this information.  However, we do have a duty placed on us by the Secretary of State’s regulations, to publish a report by 1 September 2026 on the implementation of relationships and sexuality education in grant-aided schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to planning application LA01/2017/1162/F, why the Northern Ireland Environment Agency has not responded to a consultation issued on 28 June 2023.
answer_text: In April 2023, DAERA took the decision to temporarily stop using the existing operational protocol, for considering air quality impacts from ammonia, which extended to advice provided to planning consultations for agricultural developments. This was due to concerns regarding its robustness and compliance with environmental law, in light of increasing levels of pollution, evidence of environmental damage and associated case law.	Unfortunately, the outcome was a temporary pause in issuing consultation responses until November, and again in December, as further adjustments were made, following updated information and an intervention by the Office for Environmental Protection. Consequently, the planning caseload continued to accumulate, resulting in the current backlog. My officials are now addressing this backlog and while it is not possible to provide updates on individual cases, officials have assured me cases are steadily progressing and the backlog is being actively managed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of times parents have requested sight of Relationships and Sexuality Education materials in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Individual schools are responsible for the delivery of Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) and my Department does not hold the information that you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11097/22-27, whether responsibility and decision-making on a badger cull now lies with the Secretary of State.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sat within my ministerial responsibilities and were subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.However, from 17 May 2024 the Defra Secretary of State has exercised his discretion to not retain direction and control in relation to disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health and these areas are once again under my ministerial responsibility. This includes responsibility for bovine Tuberculosis, including wildlife intervention.This discretion should be understood in the context of the Secretary of State’s criteria for retaining direction and control, which include:operational arrangements at Northern Ireland ports and airports concerning the movement of goods between GB and NI;policy developments and legislation that would impact movements between Great Britain and Northern Ireland; andnew EU laws which replace regulations under Annex II of the Windsor Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how; and (ii) with what effect departmental functions have been removed from his control; and (iii) the practical consequences of this.
answer_text: The functions of my department have not changed since the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on 12 April 2024. However, there are currently 19 pieces of EU legislation where the Secretary of State has not exercised his discretion not to exercise direction and control, that is, they are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State. A list of these has been deposited in the Assembly Library and can be accessed via the link below.https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/globalassets/documents/raise/deposited-papers/2024/dp1767.pdfIn addition, from 17 May 2024, the Defra Secretary of state has exercised discretion in the areas of disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health and these matters are now under my ministerial responsibility.This discretion should be understood in the context of the Secretary of State’s criteria for retaining direction and control, which include:operational arrangements at Northern Ireland ports and airports concerning the movement of goods between GB and NI;policy developments and legislation that would impact movements between Great Britain and Northern Ireland; andnew EU laws which replace regulations under Annex II of the Windsor Framework.As stated in paragraph 3.5 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 statutory guidance, he practical consequences of the above are that where my department is exercising functions subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State, DAERA officials should comply with decisions made by the Secretary of State as they would with those made by me. Therefore, in circumstances where officials would normally either inform or seek a decision from me on a given matter, they should advise and seek decisions from the Secretary of State in the same way and in good time. On the other hand, in circumstances where officials would not normally either inform me, or seek decisions on a given matter, they should not normally do so from the Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs where responsibility lies for the prevention and control of the animal diseases listed in (i) EU 2018/1882; and (ii) EU 2021/260.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sat within my ministerial responsibilities and were subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.However, the Defra Secretary of State has exercised his discretion to not retain direction and control in relation to disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health. As from 17 May 2024, these areas are once again under my ministerial responsibility. This includes responsibility for the prevention and control of: (i) the animal diseases listed in Regulation (EU) 2018/1882; and (ii) the aquatic diseases which Northern Ireland has approved national measures in place for, as listed in Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/260.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 11465/22-27, why Section 6 of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 is not listed as a relevant statutory provision.
answer_text: My answer to AQW 11465/22-27 gives examples of a legislative framework relevant to my Department’s retention and disposal of records policy and schedule. While the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 is not listed, the requirements set out in Section 6: Records of Meetings is practice which my departmental officials follow and once compiled, written records are retained in accordance with the Department's policy and retention and disposal schedule.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail how he will respond to A Workforce Fit for the Future: RCPGNI Retention Strategy 2024; and (ii) when this response will be submitted.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Royal College of General Practitioners ‘A Workforce Fit for the Future’ Retention Strategy and was pleased to speak at its recent launch.  I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice that are set out in the strategy and the impact of the increasing demand and workload pressures on GPs that it highlights.  My Department has taken, and will continue to focus on, actions that will help address a number of the workforce and workload challenges and recommendations set out in the Retention Strategy.  This includes the recently agreed 2024/25 General Medical Services (GMS) contract which is also an important step forward to achieving greater stability in General Practice.  The contract makes significant progress against key aims identified by GPs in their negotiations, including provision of £5million dedicated to GP indemnity, as an interim measure, pending identification of the long-term model for future provision.  We are focussing on building the GP workforce through the Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme, and increasing the number of GP training places to its current level of 121.  We are also taking steps to increase the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams (MDTs) and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists.In terms of the GP Fellowship Scheme, we are considering how the Scheme might be further developed, subject to available funding, to extend the range and scope of Fellowship placement opportunities and provide for Educational Bursaries to be available for all Fellows. 	My Department has also developed guidance which will improve management of the interface between primary and secondary care and is engaging with relevant NICS Departments and public bodies to explore ways to reduce the administrative burden on GP practices of completing non-statutory administrative tasks and the unnecessary directing of patients to GPs.My Department is committed to working to identify solutions to the issues facing General Practice, including further roll out of MDTs, exploring digital solutions and working with GPs and HSC Trusts to promote stability in General Medical Services.  However, progress on initiatives will be constrained by the availability of the necessary funding to support this. Regrettably, the 2024-25 Budget outcome for my Department was significantly less than what we required and has resulted in 2.3% less funding in 2024-25 than we spent in 2023-24.  Health will be facing significant budgetary pressures in 2024-25 with some extremely difficult decisions required to manage within the budget allocated.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) who has been commissioned to provide Irish language classes to Assembly Commission staff; and (ii) the cost associated with the provision of these classes.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Assembly Commission staff took part in a range of inter-parliamentary events as part of Learning at Work Week which ran from the 13th – 19th May 2024. The week is a national event which occurs annually to promote lifelong learning at work, co-ordinated by Campaign for Learning with employers invited to mark the week within their organisations. In collaboration with learning and development staff within the Welsh Senedd, the Scottish Parliament and the Houses of the Oireachtas, a range of virtual events were offered to all staff within each of the organisations.  It was the responsibility of each organisation to propose, host and manage virtual events in line with the annual theme for Learning at Work Week, which this year was ‘Learning Power’.  The Houses of the Oireachtas offered a range of events including a one hour ‘Introduction to the Irish Language’ session.  There were no Irish language classes hosted by the Northern Ireland Assembly Commission and no costs incurred.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department has been taken following the publication of the NI Audit Office Report, Access to General Practice in Northern Ireland, which states general practice received 5.4 per cent of the overall Health and Social Care spend in 2022-23.
answer_text: My Department welcomes the NI Audit Office’s report and is considering the report’s recommendations in full. 	As the NI Audit Office report recognises, the issues impacting general practice are complex and will require a sustained long-term response.  Specifically with respect to the issue of the proportion of health spend in general practice, my Department acknowledges that demand from general practice for an increase in the level of funding allocated to this area of health and social care.   However, my Department is also cognisant that the health and social care system necessarily operates as a whole.  Any decision to redirect money from one part of the system to another part of the system results in pressures being created in a different part of the system.  		Within the context of the significantly constrained financial position of recent years and the recent disappointing budget settlement, my Department is committed to providing investment support for general practice.  In recent months, I have approved the Dentists’ and Doctors’ Review Board (DDRB) recommendation of a 6% uplift for independent General Medical Practitioners.  In addition, I also approved an uplift of 6% for staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift (3% recurrent) for other expenses.  My Department has recently agreed the 2024/25 General Medical Services contract with NI General Practitioners Committee.  These new contract arrangements will provide GPs with greater certainty over their income throughout the year, as well as reducing administrative burdens and associated costs to practices.  These settlements recognise the pivotal role played by general practice within our health and social care system and my Department’s commitment to taking measures to improve the sustainability of general practice provision.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the cost of the portrait of the former Speaker, Lord Hay.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission produces official portraits of those who have held the offices of Speaker, First Minister and deputy First Minister.The portrait of Speaker Hay was commissioned in 2022 at a cost of £12,500.

answer_text: External engagement undertaken in the context of the Specialised Committee and the Joint Consultative Working Group’s thematic sub-groups is a matter for the UK Government and the European Union to determine, as co-chairs of the Windsor Framework bodies in question.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of their statutory obligation to represent the interests of victims, whether they intend to take action to address the legacy of the past, including condemning murders carried out by the Provisional IRA and other terrorist organisations.

answer_text: TEO officials routinely attend the Joint Consultative Working Group as part of the UK delegation. Under the rules of procedure agreed by the UK Government and EU co-chairs, the content of these meetings are confidential.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11097/22-27, to confirm that all animal health and disease eradication powers, including responsibility for tackling bovine TB and the related compensation scheme, now lie with the Secretary of State.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sat within my ministerial responsibilities and were subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.However, the Defra Secretary of State has exercised his discretion to not retain direction and control in relation to disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health. As from 17 May 2024, these areas are once again under my ministerial responsibility. This includes responsibility for tackling bovine TB and the related compensation scheme.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of whether their Department has access to sufficient information, in a timely manner, to ensure that engagement mechanisms with the EU, as referenced in the Windsor Framework, are effective as possible.
answer_text: Officials in The Executive Office regularly interact with counterparts in the UK Government on Windsor Framework-related issues, including on helping to ensure engagement mechanisms with the EU are utilised as effectively as possible. Policy officers in the Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels engage with the UK Mission to the EU and other stakeholders with the same intent.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement that has taken place between political representatives from Northern Ireland and the UK-EU structures set out in the Windsor Framework, including the Joint Committee.
answer_text: We attended the UK-EU Withdrawal Agreement Joint Committee on 16 May 2024.  Junior Ministers also attended the Trade and Cooperation Agreement Partnership Council on the same day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the number of (i) qualified classroom assistants; and (ii) units available for Key Stage 1 pupils with autism in relation to current need.
answer_text: This information is not held by the Department or the Education Authority (EA).There is a total of c112 classes available to Key Stage 1 pupils with autism, with a further 23 classes planned for September 2024.  This increase reflects the significant demand for special educational needs (SEN) placements at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 11379/22-27, to detail (i) the date the individuals ceased to be employed as screeners in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT); (ii) the reasons for them leaving their positions; and (iii) whether they are currently employed by the SHSCT, or any other branch of the NHS, as screeners or another capacity.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust has advised it does not comment on individuals or their specific circumstances. However, the Trust has confirmed that none of the screeners involved are currently working within the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme in any Trust, nor are they involved in any aspect of the cervical cytology slide review.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether there are any obligations arising in the Ministerial Code in relation to the operation of the East-West Council.
answer_text: No obligations in respect of the operation of the East-West Council are specified in the Ministerial Code.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support available to parents who have been advised that their child is likely to be diagnosed with autism, pending confirmation by a specialist assessment.
answer_text: Arrangements for autism assessment and subsequent diagnoses are a matter for the Minister of Health.  It is important to note that support in school is not dependent on a medical diagnosis of autism but based on educational need.  Parents/carers should be encouraged to speak to the class teacher and/or the school Special Educational Needs Co-ordinator (SENCO) to ensure that their child/young person is being supported within the school and receiving reasonable adjustments.There is support available from Middletown Centre for Autism https://www.middletownautism.com for education staff, parents, children and young people without any requirement for an autism diagnosis.The Education Authority (EA) also provides information, support and useful resources for special educational provision as set out in the EA Plan of Arrangements which can be found at https://send.eani.org.uk/ea-plan-arrangements.  Within this there are links to autism support through the Autism Advisory and Intervention Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the backlog of children waiting to see an educational phycologist, whether he plans to review the current policy of the Education Authority of not accepting private diagnoses of autism.
answer_text: A formal medical diagnosis of Autism can only be given by a specific group of healthcare professionals (eg paediatricians or child psychologists).As part of the statutory assessment process, the Education Authority (EA) will seek advice from the relevant Health and Social Care Trust through a designated officer. Where parents submit reports made by private professionals, the Education Authority (EA) should consider these along with the advice provided by the designated officer.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the amount spent on the purchase and installation of solar panels and similar technologies at Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: As part of the roof project in 2015, solar thermal tubes, PV panels and rain water harvesting were installed on the roof.  These items were installed as part of a major construction project and therefore a breakdown of the unit parts is not available.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the financial savings as a result of the installation of solar panels and similar technologies at Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: Building Services’ records show that between 2018 until 2020, the PV roof panels appeared to generate between around 75,000 and 160,000 kWH pa which was available for use in Parliament Buildings and which generated savings of approximately £10k - £15k per annum.The PV panels were disconnected in late 2020 due to the non-payment of the relevant grid connection fees by a sub-contractor.  Work is underway to ensure the panels are reconnected under the revised and upgraded certification requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the answer provided to AQW 23205/17-22.
answer_text: AQW 23205/17-22 was answered by my predecessor, and it is not for me to provide an update to her answer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQO 393/22-27, and in relation to the Secretary of State instructing staff in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on the additional work as a result of the implementation of the Windsor Framework, whether staff costs in the performance of these functions will be funded from the block grant or by the Northern Ireland Office and HM Treasury.
answer_text: Where additional staff are required by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to operationalise the Windsor Framework, a bid can be submitted for these costs through my Department to Treasury for consideration.Bids have been submitted to Treasury for 2024-25 based on anticipated costs as at February 2024. However, as per my previous response, Treasury is yet to confirm the available funding for implementation of the Windsor Framework for 2024-25 but I anticipate this will be agreed in time to be allocated to departments as part of the June monitoring exercise.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of comments made to the Covid-19 Inquiry by the former Head of the Northern Ireland Civil Service in relation to the capability of the Executive to analyse and understand complex issues.
answer_text: This is a matter for the Inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for comments made by the then Permanent Secretary of the Department of Health to the Covid-19 Inquiry in 2023 that systems, processes and structures for pandemic preparedness in Northern Ireland were robust and appropriate and could not be improved in any identifiable respect.
answer_text: The comments referred to in AQW 11644/22-27 whilst not verbatim, I understand they are the members’ interpretation of comments made by the former Permanent Secretary for the Department of Health (DoH), during his Module 1 evidence to the COVID-19 Inquiry at their public hearings in July 2023.Module 1 of the COVID-19 Inquiry is focussed on Resilience and Preparedness. The comments were made in the context of emergency planning systems and structures in Northern Ireland, linkages to the wider UK emergency planning arrangements, and a question from Counsel to the Inquiry in relation to whether the absence of Ministers and an Executive between 2017 and 2020 had any direct impact on DoH emergency planning and preparedness.   The transcript from the relevant evidence session is publicly available via the UK COVID Inquiry website (https://covid19.public-inquiry.uk/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/11163324/C-19-Inquiry-11-July-23-Module-1-Day-18.pdf).I look forward to considering the Chair’s report for Module 1 which is due to be published summer 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail why hand-written notes of the 2 July 2020 Executive meeting were not provided to the Covid-19 Inquiry until last week, despite being available from August 2023.
answer_text: The Executive Office has commissioned an independent investigation of this matter, the results of which have been provided to the Inquiry.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how the evidence provided to the Covid-19 Inquiry advising that records had been wiped complies with Section 6 of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: Formal minutes of all Executive meetings are compiled in keeping with the requirements of Section 6 of the Act.  The minutes of all such meetings that took place during the period covered by the Covid-19 Public Inquiry have been disclosed to the Inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of how the statutory guidance, issued by the Secretary of State in relation to the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, will impact on the ordering and control of finances, having regard to the specific guidance on managing public money and resourcing.
answer_text: The statutory guidance that accompanied the Regulations states that the Regulations will operate consistently with the Statement of Funding Policy. The costs of implementing the Windsor framework are provided by the Treasury to the Executive on a ring-fenced basis. Departments submit bids to the Treasury, through the Department of Finance, twice a year for this purpose. As the funding is ring-fenced it cannot be used for any other purpose. The guidance also states that if any budgetary or headcount issues emerge these should be raised with the Department of Finance in the usual way and that the Secretary of State will seek to resolve issues arising with departments and Treasury, as necessary, to support the operation of the Windsor Framework. The Department of Finance’s role is to continue to liaise with all departments to understand any financial impacts that have arisen as a result of implementation of the Framework and, where appropriate, to make the case to Treasury for the additional funding.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) when; and (ii) how the Secretary of State has engaged with Executive Ministers on the operation of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.
answer_text: The UK Government notified the local parties and NICS departments in advance of laying the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024. We as First Minister and deputy First Minister were briefed as part of this process. We cannot answer for other Executive Ministers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why his Department’s cross-compliance guidance includes the wording, you must observe the testing frequency in the legislation for bovine Tuberculosis, without specifying what the required frequencies and gradations are.
answer_text: The Cross-Compliance Verifiable Standards document sets out the requirements that must be met by all farmers claiming certain area-based schemes to avoid any reduction to their payments.  It explains what farmers can expect if selected for inspection and provides help and guidance to assist farmers in meeting these requirements.  It is an advisory document and is not intended to set out the penalties which can result from non-compliance with any of the requirements.  Statutory Management Requirement (SMR) 4 (Food and Feed Law) includes the requirement that keepers of animals must observe the testing frequency set out in the legislation for Tuberculosis or other diseases.  The relevant legislation is set out in correspondence from the Department advising herd keepers of the date on which they should present animals for inspection.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) her Department’s policy on the retention and disposal of records; (ii) when the policy was initiated; (iii) when the policy was last revised; and (iv) any breaches of the policy that have occurred.
answer_text: (i) The Department of Justice’s Retention and Disposal policy is published on the DOJ website as ‘DoJ Retention and Disposal Schedules version 4’ (2016). question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his Department’s policy on the retention and disposal of records; (ii) when the policy was initiated; (iii) when the policy was last revised; and (iv) any breaches of the policy that have occurred.
answer_text: My Department's retention and disposal policy is in line with NICS practice to provide a system to maintain, retain or dispose of its records in accordance with legislative and administrative requirements to support business functions and activities for as long as they are required.The legislative framework includes the Public Records Act (Northern Ireland) 1923; the Disposal of Documents Order 1925; the UK General Data Protection Regulation 2016; the Data Protection Act 2018; the Environmental Information Regulations 2004 and section 12 of the Lord Chancellor’s Code of Practice on the management of records issued under Section 46 of the Freedom of Information Act 2000, which states that:‘Authorities should define how long they need to keep particular records, should dispose of them when they are no longer needed and should be able to explain why records are no longer held.’When records are identified for disposal, authorisation is sought from Information Asset Owners, at Principal grade or higher, and when obtained, the documents are disposed of, by destruction or transfer to the Public Record Office of Northern Ireland (PRONI).  (ii) when the policy was initiated; 28 July 2016(iii) when the policy was last revised; 13 Nov 2023(iv) any breaches of the policy that have occurred. None recorded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the guidance that outlines that a delay of 98 days or more in bovine Tuberculosis testing can result in the delay being deemed intentional with enhanced penalty; and (ii) how this is conveyed to (a) farmers; and (b) veterinary practitioners.
answer_text: (i) There is no guidance which sets out the late Tuberculosis (TB) testing penalty matrix.  It is a legislative requirement that all herd keepers must test in line with the TB testing programme requirements.  Specifically, they must test by the date notified by the Department in order to avoid penalties.(ii) a.  Herd Keepers are advised in test notification letters issued by the Department that non-adherence to testing requirements may result in penalties of up to 100% of all payments due.   (ii) b.  A contract is in place between the Department and Private Veterinary Practitioners (PVPs) which includes the provision of bovine TB testing.  Paragraph 6.11 of Schedule 1 of the contract states:Keepers may be subjected to Cross Compliance penalties for failure to complete TB tests on time, so if the Contractor is unable to fulfil their obligations and notifies both their local DVO and TB Contracts, alternative arrangements can be made.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the sale of new cars in Northern Ireland from July 2024, to detail any differences, including the reason for such differences, that will exist to the required specification for intelligent speed assistance compared to Great Britain.
answer_text: I consider any measure that will help promote road safety to be positive, particularly in light of the high number of people being killed and injured on our roads.  You may be aware that excess speed for the conditions is a factor in many fatal collisions, and anything that helps address this, such as Intelligent Speed Assistance (ISA), is to be welcomed.ISA will become mandatory for all new cars sold in the EU and here from July 2024.  The vast majority of cars sold in England, Scotland and Wales from then are likely to be fitted with identical ISA technology, which will be used in exactly the same way.As with any driver assistance system the driver will be able to switch it off, but it will automatically come back on when the vehicle is restarted. ISA makes the driver aware of the current speed limit.  It can provide a warning if the limit is exceeded or may control the vehicle’s speed to remain within the limit. The driver can always override the system, so it does not prevent them from speeding (i.e. it is not a speed limiter). One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the 2024-25 Barnett consequential allocation in relation to childcare provision in England and Wales.
answer_text: The Executive received £57.2 million of Barnett consequentials for 2024-25, following the announcement in the Spring Budget of the expansion of the 30 free hours of childcare scheme in England. Barnett consequentials are unhypothecated, meaning they do not have to be used for the purposes for which they were issued, but rather allocated in line with the Executive’s priorities.  The 2024-25 Budget provided £25m to support the development of a Childcare Strategy. The Executive may increase the funding for this in-year when proposals are brought forward by the cross-departmental task and finish group established by the Education Minister to take forward the development of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of attacks on Orange Halls in each of the last five years; and (ii) the number of people convicted as a result of these attacks.
answer_text: Information on the numbers of attacks on Orange and Apprentice Boys Halls is published on the PSNI website periodically, as part of the information contained in their hate crime statistics. A PSNI control strategy for attacks on symbolic buildings is in place across all Districts.  The police continue to work in partnership with the Orange Order and other community organisations in an effort to prevent such attacks and, where they occur, to exploit all evidential opportunities to bring those responsible to justice.PSNI data on attacks on symbolic buildings is compiled centrally with Orange Halls grouped alongside Apprentice Boys Halls and relate to crimes where a sectarian motivation has been recorded, where the premises is the intended target of the attack, and where the premises is an Orange Hall or Apprentice Boys Hall. Attacks on Orange and Apprentice Boys Halls 2018/19 – 2022/23Financial year Number of attacks on Orange Halls / Apprentice Boys Halls2018/19 102019/20 142020/21 142021/22 122022/23 19Information on offences reported to the police and associated statistics, including those on the number of Orange Halls that have been attacked in each of the last five years, is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any further query relating to the issues raised in part (i) of the question to the PSNI.In relation to part (ii) of the question, Departmental prosecutions and convictions datasets do not contain information on the type of building, or the address, at which an offence occurred and there is no specific offence related to an attack on Orange Halls.  Attacks on such buildings may be prosecuted under more generic ‘criminal damage’, ‘theft’ or ‘arson’ type offence descriptions. Therefore, from information held, the Department is not able to provide the information requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have been prescribed drugs to suppress the release of sex hormones in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of people who have been prescribed drugs to suppress the release of sex hormones is not available; if a patient does not take a prescription to a dispenser and get it dispensed, then the data cannot be collected.  The total number of patients dispensed products that suppress the release of sex hormones in primary care in NI, for each year relating to the period from January 2019 to December 2023, (the latest period for which information is publicly available), is provided in the table overleaf.  The Business Services Organisation processes prescription information but does not hold any information on the indication for which drugs have been prescribed.  Drugs prescribed to suppress the release of sex hormones in individuals are licensed by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency for the treatment of a range of conditions including prostatic hyperplasia, prostate cancer, gonadotrophin-dependent precocious puberty and endometriosis.  Healthcare professionals may also prescribe medicines beyond the scope of the MHRA licence in certain circumstances.  For example, the use of triptorelin for gender dysphoria is outside the terms defined by the licence (off-label).The total number of patients dispensed products that suppress the release of sex hormones, in primary care in NI, 2019 - 20231,2,3YearNumber of Patients201925,255202025,070202125,690202226,621202327,330Source: Family Practitioner Services Pharmacy Payment System, Family Practitioner Services, HSC Business Services Organisation1 All information is for prescriptions prescribed in Primary care and dispensed by a community pharmacist, dispensing doctor or appliance contractor. Data on secondary care prescribing and private prescriptions are not held by the Business Services Organisation.2 A range of medicines may be used to suppress sex hormones. Information has been provided for the chemical names bicalutamide, buserelin, cyproterone, dutasteride, enzalutamide, finasteride, goserelin, leuprorelin, nafarelin, triptorelin and tamsulosin + dutasteride.3 The number of patients is the number of estimated unique patients whose dispensed scripts have been successfully matched against the central GP patient register in each time period.  If a patient has one or more prescriptions, the patient will be counted once.  There is a chance that some patients may have been missed completely if all of the scripts dispensed to them were not successfully scanned. However, because patients will generally have been dispensed multiple prescriptions over the course of a year, there is a high chance they will have been picked up at least once in any given year, the exception being those newly prescribed the drug towards year end.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail all financial savings identified in his Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government, impacting right across its essential services. Following my appointment as Minister for Infrastructure I took immediate action in 2023-24 to begin to address the continued deterioration in our road network. I was able to provide additional funding in year to start to address the underinvestment in our roads through a combination of additional funding from the Executive supplemented by a reallocation within my Department. I am currently considering the outcome of the 2024-25 budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail all financial savings identified in her Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: My Department started the 2023-24 financial year with a forecast overspend of £149m. Financial savings through efficiencies were achieved both prior to and after 3 February 2024. This included measures such as vacancy management, pausing recruitment, stopping all discretionary spend, and not commencing new areas of spend. Although efficiencies were made, my Department could not live within the opening 2023-24 resource budget and subsequently required a further £86m allocation in year to ensure that it did not overspend.In relation to 2024-25 I am currently considering what actions are required as the resource budget allocated to my Department falls significantly short of what is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to detail all financial savings identified in her Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: Given the 2024-25 Budget allocations have been confirmed, my Department, as with others, face a challenge to prioritise and deliver services within the money allocated. Financial savings and income generating options had been developed by officials in advance of the Budget settlement and these are being reviewed in the context of the Budget outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail all financial savings identified in his Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: Significant work is being taken forward across Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland to reduce costs and improve productivity and efficiency. However, whilst this work will deliver better services for our citizens  , the majority of it will not result in financial savings due to the impact of rising demand..The following are examples of some of the large scale projects operating across all of Health and Social Care that have had my particular attention since my return to office but I can assure you that, given the financial constraints we are operating under, in addition to the many regional projects, schemes are also underway in each Trust.Reducing agency spend has been a key priority of mine.  As a result of the work I requested, for 2023/24 there is an overall projected net reduction in agency spend in the region of £20m and a reduction of over £80m in off-framework spend.  Ongoing reform, innovation and capacity building within elective care has resulted in a sustained reduction in the number of patients waiting for inpatient and day case admission; since 31 December 2022 the treatment waiting list is down 14.4%.In addition, the NHS England Get it Right First Time (GIRFT) team has undertaken reviews of our Emergency Departments and Urology and Gynaecology service to maximise capacity and productivity in the system and to ensure a sustainable service and effective patient throughput.  A programme of service configuration reviews are underway to further improve efficiency including Workforce, Urgent and Emergency Care, Digital, Cancer, Social Care and Primary Care.  Each of these workstreams are overseen by a Programme Board with the Performance and Transformation Board (PTEB) providing overarching governance.The Delivering Value Programme Board has been established to bring together the efficiency and productivity gains of existing programmes of work and to identify further areas that could generate either cash releasing savings or productivity gains.  As part of this work, GIRFT are also undertaking a piece of work to identify opportunities for further savings across the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail all financial savings identified in his Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: My Department is in a similar position to where it was at the start of last year despite the significant increase to the level of the opening budget shortfall.  In 2023-24 the Department took significant, difficult decisions to reduce spend.  Over the last number of years, the Department has had to identify programmes and areas of spend that could be scaled back or stopped.  In most instances, the financial efficiencies achieved have been extremely limited, relative to the scale of pressures previously or indeed, now faced.  My Department is very restricted in its ability to reprioritise or reduce spend due to the vast majority of its budget going to meet statutory and demand-led obligations, not to mention the fact that about 80% of the total Education budget is subsumed by staff costs.  Significant change to my Department’s budget footprint would require, in many instances, quite significant change to legislation. Since 3 February 2024, my focus has been on building on Northern Ireland’s reputation for providing a high-quality education system. Throughout the recent 2024-25 Executive Budget process and in the future I will continue strongly advocate for both the investment and transformation required to deliver the first-class education system that all our children in Northern Ireland deserve.   My Department will work constructively with the Department of Finance and other Executive departments more widely to develop a programme of sustainability measures, which is a key requirement of the financial settlement which accompanied the restoration of Northern Ireland Executive.The 2024-25 Budget outcome for Education leaves me with serious concerns that without significant additional funding in-year, my Department will not be able to deliver basic statutory services without overspending.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail all financial savings identified in his Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: My Department is facing a challenging 2024-25 Budget position. An exercise is currently underway to consider how my Department lives within its 2024-25 Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail all financial savings identified in his Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: In order to live with DAERA’s agreed Resource DEL budget for the financial year to 31 March 2024 financial savings were identified before 3 February 2024.  		Further work has taken place since 3 February 2024 to identify financial savings that could help DAERA live within its agreed Resource DEL allocation for 2024-25.  My Department is currently firming up these savings in light of the Resource DEL allocation that was confirmed for DAERA by the Executive on 25 April 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to detail all financial savings identified in their Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: The Executive Office is committed to the delivery of financial savings through efficiency measures and ensuring that value for money is secured through all activities and plans.  2023-24 Spending Plans are published on the TEO website. As we develop financial plans for 2024-25, especially in the continuing constrained budget environment, we are committed to delivering savings through efficiencies and by identifying and delivering opportunities for transformation.  This will include reviewing our activities, plans and structures in order to ensure that all opportunities for efficiencies are identified to ensure best value is achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail all financial savings identified in his Department through efficiencies since 3 February 2024; and (ii) what programme of work is in place to address this issue.
answer_text: The Secretary of State’s actions in setting the 2022-23 and 2023-24 budgets, coupled with the loss of EU funding, and rising costs have had a significant detrimental impact on my Department’s budget.  You will be aware that before I came into post, the Department for the Economy had commenced a voluntary exit scheme for Further Education Colleges.  It is estimated this will deliver circa £12 million of financial savings annually.In the period since 3 February 2024 my focus has been on delivering my Economic Vision to ensure our economy begins to grow.  In delivering my Economic Vision I am clear that funding must be used effectively.  I am currently considering how the budget allocated to my Department for 2024-25 can be maximised to deliver the best outcomes for our economy and our citizens.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) what research has been conducted on an individual’s appointment to other quangos at the end of their term; (ii) what steps have been taken to minimise an individual from being appointed to other quangos when their term ends; and (iii) whether their Department will ask the Commissioner for Public Appointments, when appointed, to address this matter.
answer_text: Public appointment statistics are published by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency in the Public Appointments Annual Report(which is available on The Executive Office website at:  https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/topics/public-appointments-reports ). The Report includes statistics on multiple appointments, setting out how many individuals hold appointments concurrently. The press release announcing a public appointment is also required to include information on any other public appointments held by an appointee.The overarching principle governing public appointments is selection on merit. While an individual is not restricted from being appointed to the Board of another public body at the end of their term, considerations such as conflicts of interest, probity, and an individual candidate’s ability to commit the required time to the role, form part of the selection process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what progress has been made in (i) resolving; and (ii) paying out the holiday pay claims to PSNI officers and prison officers, including the backdating of the pay claim.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) implemented a temporary arrangement on holiday pay with effect from 1 April 2020. This ensures that staff are paid “normal remuneration” when taking the four weeks annual leave stipulated within the Working Time Regulations, including overtime and claimable allowances. Staff automatically receive a holiday pay payment in their salaries when any of the relevant payments are processed on the NIPS payroll system.A number of prison officers lodged claims with the Industrial Tribunal. In line with the approach taken by the wider Northern Ireland Civil Service, an offer was put forward to settle these claims, which is currently with the respective solicitors for consideration. As these cases remain before the Tribunal, it would not be appropriate to comment further.  Holiday pay claims to PSNI officers are a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.   I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Board in holding him to account.  You may wish to seek an update directly from the PSNI.However, my Department continues to work with and support the PSNI on the development of a holiday pay business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the renewable energy project at Causeway Hospital will go live.
answer_text: The Causeway Hospital Solar PV system is forecasted to become operational during January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the considerations that prevail when a contractor is engaged under the Early Contractor Involvement arrangements and the contract is delayed by more than a decade; (ii) in what circumstances would fresh procurement arise; and (iii) to provide the relevant guidance relating to this.
answer_text: Early Contractor Involvement (ECI) is a two-stage arrangement that typically comprises a development stage; and, if the prescribed conditions are met, a construction stage. (i) 	If a project is delayed in the development stage, key considerations are the Public Contracts Regulations 2015(as amended) (PCR) and the terms of the ECI arrangement.(ii)	A fresh procurement may arise if the contract is no longer valid, or the terms of the contract that must be satisfied to progress to the construction phase have not been met, or the PCR cannot be complied with.(iii)	The PCR and associated case law guide any such decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether the A5 preferred route passes through any flood plains; and (ii) the anticipated impact for established housing in the area.
answer_text: Appendix 16-1 of the Environmental Assessment Addendum 2022 explains how the A5 project crosses 25 flood plains over its full 85 kilometre length. The Flood Risk Assessment (FRA) and its findings, discussed at length during the Public Inquiry held in May and June 2023, identifies existing flood risk receptors (including housing) and takes them into account when classifying flood risk associated with the project. Housing is classified as a high importance flood receptor. The number of properties assessed as being at potential flood risk and the associated mitigation measures are given in Volume 3 of the FRA. Volume 3 also explains measures used to mitigate the impacts of the project. The FRA can be viewed at: A5WTC ESA2022 Appendix 16-1 Flood Risk Assessment - Optimised | A5 Western Transport Corridor Hub Site.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, further to paragraph 15(1) of Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016 as amended by the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) (Amendment) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2020, to detail (i) whether an independent MP, by virtue of suspension from their party, is regarded as being a member of the same party as an MLA with whom they share a constituency office for the purposes of this determination, and the rationale for this decision; and (ii) the impact on an MLA’s eligibility for allowances, including rent and rates, as
answer_text: Whether a person is a member of a political party is a private law matter between the individual and the party in question.  Where suspension of party membership means that an individual is still deemed to be a member of the political party, albeit with no active benefits in place or with the Whip removed, the individual may still be regarded as being a ‘member’ of the same political party for the purposes of this Determination.Where this applies, the definitions of shared offices, as set out in paragraph 15 of the Determination and with regards to the recovery of rent, still apply.  A suspension from a political party does not automatically impact on the level of rent that may be claimed as part of an office which is shared (for the purposes of the Determination).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the approved 2023-24 minor works road schemes for North Antrim.
answer_text: My Department had no minor works schemes programmed in the North Antrim area for the financial year 2023-24.  However, essential works were completed through the Local Transport and Safety Measures programme to replace traffic signals at: Curran Road/Main Street/ junction, Larne; Agnew Street/Victoria Road junction, Larne; Parkhall College junction, Antrim; and, A26 Newbridge Road at the Causeway Hospital in Coleraine. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when her Department will conclude its deliberations on the business case presented by the Department for the Economy on a revised non-domestic RHI tariff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the non-domestic RHI tariff that would be required to give the promised 12 per cent return.
answer_text: The 2019 tariffs for small and medium biomass installations were based on evidence within a report by the energy consultancy firm, Ricardo Energy and Environmental (https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/consultations/future-northern-ireland-non-domestic-renewable-heat-incentive-scheme), and set at a level to provide a typical 12% prospective annual internal rate of return over the remainder of the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme.I am working towards securing the agreement of the Executive to close the Non-Domestic RHI Scheme. A tariff review will be considered alongside closure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will deliver on the declared intention of his Department's Permanent Secretary to the NI Affairs Committee on 12 March 2024, to bring forward secondary legislation to revise the non-domestic RHI tariff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the percentage of rates raised that has been spent on staff wages in each of the last six years, broken down by local council.
answer_text: Councils are separate legal entities and are responsible for their own budgets and district rates. As the amount that a council spends on staff wages is an operational matter for each council, you should direct your question to each individual council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has submitted a business case to the Department of Finance providing for a revised non-domestic RHI tariff.
answer_text: A business case was submitted to Department of Finance in relation to a revised non-domestic RHI tariff for small and lower medium biomass installations in November 2023. However, following the return of the Executive in February 2024, any tariff review must now be considered in the context of closure of the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme.  I am working towards securing the agreement of the Executive to close the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in relation to proposed legislation on defective premises limitation periods, whether he intends to include a provision whereby a case previously dismissed by reason of limitation restrictions can be relitigated.
answer_text: It is not intended to extend provisions of the proposed legislation to cases which have been finally determined by a court, whether on the basis of limitation or otherwise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 9625/22-27, (i) whether he is asserting no consultation was held, given the Explanatory Memorandum accompanying the Windsor Framework Regulations 2024 claims the government consulted with the relevant ministers; (ii) in the absence of a formal consultation, whether there was an informal or any other type of consultation; and (iii) to detail the response of his Department.
answer_text: I can confirm that as Minister I did not have an opportunity to input into any formal consultative process with relation to the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024. I and my officials were given opportunity to comment on the statutory guidance referenced in regulation 4 and also on the extent to which Secretary of State might exercise his discretion as provided for in regulation 3.  In providing comment, my aim was to raise relevant concerns, ensure clarity on the scope of the regulations and on how they might work in operation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given his local council oversight role, whether he will examine if the street sign policies of some councils are compliant with the requirements of Section 78F(2) of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: I understand that Section 78F(2) of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 is the national and cultural identity principles and, when commenced, is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Office and will place a duty on public authorities including Councils. It would not, therefore, be for the Department for Communities to examine if the street sign policies of some councils are compliant with the requirements of Section 78F(2) of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how far the street sign policies of some councils, based on meeting 15 per cent demand, take account of the sensitivities of those with different national and cultural identities, as required by Section 78F(2) of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: I understand that Section 78F(2) of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 is the national and cultural identity principles and, when commenced, is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Office and will place a duty on public authorities including Councils. It would not, therefore, be for the Department for Communities to assess how councils take account of the sensitivities of those with different national and cultural identities, as required by Section 78F(2).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any instructions that have been issued by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland to his Department under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.
answer_text: The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 provide for functions of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs that relate to observing or implementing a relevant provision of the Windsor Framework to be exercised subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.While the Secretary of State has confirmed the areas where he has decided to exercise discretion not to exercise direction and control, I understand that the Department has not to date received any specific instructions on how, for the remaining areas, the Secretary of State wishes officials to take forward their statutory obligations.I raised the Member’s request that members are informed when directions are issued under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 when meeting Minister of State Rt. Hon. Steve Baker MP on Tuesday 23 April 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: My Department notes the recent publication of the Cass Review, which was commissioned by NHS England. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has plans to include a provision in the hate crime bill, making a description of another person’s biological sex a criminal offence.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the legal consequences of a draft Budget not being laid, pursuant to the mandatory requirement of Section 64(1) of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.
answer_text: The timescales for agreeing a Budget for 2024-25 are far from ideal but it is important that the Executive fully considers the Budget it is agreeing. The Executive has agreed a short delay until the end of April to allow for this. Section 64 of the NI Act 1998 also requires that the Minister of Finance must lay a statement in the Assembly setting out the funding available to the Executive as notified by the Secretary of State. This must be done 14 days before laying a Budget. As I only received confirmation of the Executive’s funding from the Secretary of State on 20 March, this requirement in itself prevented me from laying a Budget before the start of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps are being taken to bring pay equity to pharmacists attached to GP practices under multidisciplinary teams and to effect Agenda for Change banding changes.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the various issues regarding remuneration for GP practice pharmacists employed by GP Federations which you have raised. I can assure you that such issues are already subject to ongoing discussions between the Department of Health (DoH) and key stakeholders.The GP Pharmacist Oversight Board was established by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the DoH in December 2023. The group brings together representatives from all key stakeholder groups, including SPPG, DoH, GP Federations and GPC (General Practitioners Committee) to facilitate collaborative working to optimise the working arrangements of the GPP workforce, in line with strategic priorities of the DoH Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework. The group will oversee the delivery of the service and facilitate discussion and resolution to issues relating to the GPP workforce employed in General Practices. The group also aims to develop the General Practice Pharmacist workforce, in line with DoH strategic direction and the Pharmacy Workforce Review, which is being progressed through Service Delivery and Service Development subgroups.DoH will continue to work closely with stakeholders over the following months to address the issues to which this question refers.
