answer_text: Details of the control period form part of the Funding Agreement which is a commercially sensitive document, therefore, both it and its contents are exempt from disclosure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will bring forward legislation to amend the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 to reflect the reforms introduced by the Mental Capacity (Amendment) Act 2019.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (MCA) received royal assent in May 2016 and has been implemented in a phased approach. Phase one was introduced between October 2019 and December 2019, with the most notable provisions commenced being the Deprivation of Liberty (DoL) Safeguards, a new statutory process for deprivation of liberty. The DoL Safeguards provide a ‘person centred’ legal framework to allow a deprivation of liberty to be carried out with regards to a person who lacks capacity, ensuring that the person’s human rights are protected.The Mental Capacity (Amendment) Act 2019 amends the Mental Capacity Act 2005 in England and Wales to reform the process for authorising arrangements enabling the care or treatment of people who lack capacity to consent to arrangements which amount to a deprivation of their liberty. The new Liberty Protection Safeguards will replace the existing DoL Safeguards system in England and Wales, putting the rights and wishes of those people at the centre of all decision-making. It is believed that that the new framework will be more aligned with the Northern Ireland MCA DoL Safeguards. However, the provisions have yet to be commenced and an implementation date remains unknown.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the milestones set out in the June 2022 Stroke Action Plan which have been (a) met; and (b) missed; and (ii) what his Department is doing to address this.
answer_text: (i) The Stroke Action Plan set out six priorities for stroke reform and the timescales for implementation of those priorities. It has not been possible to fully deliver on the Action Plan priorities within the timescale originally envisaged due to capacity and funding constraints.  However, important progress has been made. This includes:Priority One - Prevention: The development of a regional model for Transient Ischaemic Attack (TIA - mini-stroke);Priority Two - Thrombolysis: The completion of an audit which identifies the primary reasons why patients did not receive thrombolysis and sets out recommendations for improvement;Priority Three - Thrombectomy: A costed model identifying requirements to support the expansion of the thrombectomy to a 24/7 service has been developed. In addition, Artificial Intelligence software in stroke imaging has been introduced to support the identification of eligibility for thrombolysis and thrombectomy;Priority Four – Rehabilitation and Long Term Support: A regional model for Long Term Support and the Community Integrated Stroke Service has been developed. A specification for rehabilitation and long-term support has been developed;Priority Five – Hyperacute Stroke Care: A new process has now been agreed to identify a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care. Initial work, including updated modelling, is underway;Priority Six – Workforce Review: Initial work on stakeholder mapping has been undertaken. It is intended that the Workforce Review will formally commence in the Autumn.  (ii) We will continue to make every effort to progress the Action Plan priorities by making the best use of current resources. It should be noted however that there are some priorities that cannot be progressed much further, such as the expansion of the thrombectomy service, without additional funding.  In this context, the absence of adequate funding will continue to impact the pace of implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will support the granting of the Wider Service Medal to members of the Northern Ireland Security Guard Service.
answer_text: Members of the Northern Ireland Security Guard Service are Ministry of Defence (MoD) employees who provide security at and within MoD establishments in Northern Ireland. The granting of a service medal is therefore a matter for the MOD to consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how his ministerial powers are impacted by the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.
answer_text: The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 apply only to Article 5, 6 or 7 or Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework and any other provision of that Framework or any other part of the EU withdrawal agreement so far as it applies in relation to those Articles or that Annex.Where, as provided for in regulation 3, the Secretary of State has decided to exercise discretion, my ministerial powers are unaffected and I remain accountable to the Assembly for the discharge of those ministerial responsibilities.  For all other areas covered by the Regulations, it now falls to the Secretary of State and not to me to exercise direction and control.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) how he intends to address the lack of regulation in the funeral care sector; and (ii) whether he has any plans to follow steps taken by the Scottish government.
answer_text: My department has no remit regarding regulation of the funeral care sector, beyond the interest of the Trading Standards Service, which is solely from a consumer protection perspective and is covered under existing consumer protection legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs,  in consequence of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, how impacted staff in his Department will operate in terms of answerability to both him and the Secretary of State.
answer_text: The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 apply only to Article 5, 6 or 7 or Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework and any other provision of that Framework or any other part of the EU withdrawal agreement so far as it applies in relation to those Articles or that Annex.Where, as provided for in regulation 3, the Secretary of State has decided to exercise discretion, civil servants in my Department continue to be subject to my direction and control. For all other areas covered by the Regulations, civil servants working in relevant areas now must advise and take direction from the Secretary of State.With respect to articles 5-7 and Annex II of the Windsor Framework, my officials will work under my direction and control for the areas where the Secretary of State has exercised discretion under Regulation 3(3) of The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024. For the areas where discretion has not been exercised, officials will work under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of the provisions in The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, how he proposes to meet his obligations on the provision of information under the Ministerial Code and Pledge of Office.
answer_text: I will continue to ensure that, for aspects of the Windsor Framework that fall within my direction and control, all reasonable requests for information from the Assembly, users of services and individual citizens are complied with, in line with my obligations on the provision of information and principle of openness under the Ministerial Code and Pledge of Office.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will publish his Department's consultation response to the UK government ahead of the making and laying of The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.
answer_text: The UK Government did not formally consult with my Department on the making and laying of The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how the arrangements for financial support for parents of children with terminal illness in Northern Ireland differ from those in other parts of the UK; (ii) to detail the reasons for any differences; and (iii) what he is doing to address the situation.
answer_text: I know the challenges faced by parents who have a child with a terminal illness are extensive, this can impact significantly on all aspects of life including having a financial impact. Families who have children with a terminal illness have access to financial support from the Department of Health in the form of Self-Directed Support. Alongside Self-Directed Support, the Department of Communities may provide financial assistance for children who have a terminal illness through Disability Living Allowance and depending on circumstances other financial supports. Self-Directed Support is a term used to describe the ways in which social care services and support can be offered to individuals and families.  It gives greater control to the person who needs social care and to those close to them, to ensure they can shape the support they need to suit their lives. Individuals and families then have the freedom to choose the type of support they want to in order to meet their assessed needs, where they want it and when.An example of this would be in being able to arrange support staff to visit at a time that suits the family, or enabling them to employ their own carers. Families who use self-directed support are in a position to choose how their support is provided and have as much control as they want over their personal budget.A personal budget is a sum of money identified by Trusts to meet the assessed needs of a person who is eligible for social care support services. At the centre of every personal budget is an individual support plan developed by the individual and/or their representative. This support plan outlines the agreed personal goals and outcomes to meet the individuals assessed need. Once a support plan is agreed by the Trusts, the individual and/or their representative will decide how the personal budget will be managed. The individual’s personal budget can be used in any of the following four options:		A Direct Payment		A Managed budget		The Trust can arrange a service (Trust Arranged)		A mixture of options 1-3In answer to parts (ii) and (iii) of your question unfortunately I have no information in relation to what financial support is available in other jurisdictions for parents of children with a terminal illness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is designated as the competent authority under the Official Controls Regulation EU 2017/625.
answer_text: DAERA and the Food Standards Agency are designated as the central competent authorities under the Official Controls Regulation (EU) 2017/625. Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, implementation of the Official Controls Regulation (EU) 2017/625 is now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of rising costs, what support his Department will provide to ensure local agricultural shows can be held this year.
answer_text: I would like to set out my appreciation of the vital role that local agricultural shows play in our communities.  They are an integral part of the fabric of rural life, building cohesion and providing our rural communities with a focal point.  		As you are aware the Northern Ireland Executive is not yet in a position to agree a budget for 2024-25. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 7961/22-27, to detail the number of staff working in the Coleraine office, broken down by community background.
answer_text: There are currently 20.5 FTE (full-time equivalent) staff members / 22 employees (including part-time staff members) in Tourism Ireland’s Coleraine office. There are 12 from the Roman Catholic background and 10 from the Protestant background.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the (i) UK legislation; and (ii) Assembly legislation, which impacts the work of his Department.

Therefore, this list may not be exhaustive.A full list of the legislation identified in each category has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) what implementing steps; and (ii) any other actions his Department has taken in relation to (a) Council Regulation (EC) No 116/2009; (b) Directive 2014/60/EU; and (c) Council Directive 75/324/EEC.
answer_text: EU and UK export licences for objects of cultural interest from Northern Ireland are issued by Arts Council England. The Department for Culture, Media and Sport have identified ‘The Return of Cultural Objects Regulations 1994’ as EU retained law and intend to maintain their current approach. My Department has therefore not identified any steps or any actions required in relation to Council Regulation (EC) No 116/2009 or Directive 2014/60/EU.Council Directive 75/324/EEC on the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to aerosol dispensers is considered as product standards and is not a devolved matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will reform the pay and grading structure for education support service workers.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 9211/22-27.This answer will be contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 29 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister why (i) AQW 7025/22-27; and (ii) AQW 7318/22-27 have not been answered.
answer_text: AQW 7318/22-27 was answered on 22 March 2024 and AQW 7025/22-27 was answered on 29 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many libraries have specific sections in their book selection dedicated to transgender children and teenagers; and (ii) to detail the rationale for this position.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Libraries NI.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “None of our 96 public libraries have specific sections in their book selection dedicated to transgender children and teenagers.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Libraries NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of their statutory obligation to represent the interests of victims, whether they intend to take action following the publication of Operation Kenova’s interim report, including condemning the PIRA murders referenced within it.
answer_text: Our department has been representing the interests of victims and survivors for many years, and we remain committed to ensuring that the needs of victims and survivors continue to be understood and met in an appropriate way.A consultation has recently closed on the new draft Strategy for Victims and Survivors. We will shortly be considering the consultation report and any suggested amends to the draft Strategy. A key part of the Strategy will be providing support for victims and survivors, and their families, as they move forward from experiences of the past. The draft Strategy recognises the importance of truth and justice to victims and survivors, and the need to work towards greater recognition of the hurt, loss, and trauma of our past.   						We will continue to support victims and survivors in dealing with the legacy of the past, and will take a flexible approach so that we can deal with changing needs and prevailing circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) when his Department’s task and finish group will respond to the Education and Training Inspectorate's thematic evaluation of the preventative curriculum, of which Relationships and Sexuality Education is a part; and (ii) when he will issue further guidance on Relationships and Sexuality Education in schools.
answer_text: Through the task and finish group, my Department is currently considering its response to the Education and Training Inspectorate’s evaluation of the Preventative Curriculum in Schools and Education Other Than at School (EOTAS) Centres, including actions required to be taken by key delivery partners to address the report’s findings.My officials will continue to engage with key stakeholders, schools and young people as part of this process.  Guidance for schools will then be considered. It is anticipated that any additional or updated guidance will be published later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to the to AQW 5804/22-27, whether the expeditious consideration of the Guidance for Schools, EOTAS Centre and Youth Service on Supporting Transgender Young People been concluded.
answer_text: I have received briefing from my officials on the existing policy and legislative context in Northern Ireland, other UK jurisdictions and the Republic of Ireland.  I will consider this shortly, before determining any further action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) those from his Department, or in connection with his Department, that travelled to the United States of America at a cost to the public purse for St Patrick's Day events in March 2024; and (ii) the overall cost to the public purse, broken down by (a) travel; (b) accommodation; and (c) other expenses.
answer_text: My Departmental officials and I travelled to the United States of America on an international trade and investment mission and were not there for St. Patrick’s Day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the process, including checks and documentary requirements, for an individual from Northern Ireland to bring a budgie from Great Britain to Northern Ireland; and (ii) the costs associated with this process.
answer_text: (i) An individual owner travelling with a captive bird such as a budgie, where the movement does not involve any change of ownership, including rehoming or gifting, can bring the pet budgie from Great Britain (GB) to Northern Ireland (NI) under ‘The Non-Commercial Movement of Pet Animals Order (Northern Ireland) 2011’, implementing Regulation (EU) 576/2013 in NI.The pet bird must be accompanied by a valid export health certificate and written declaration as required under Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2021/1938 to ensure protection measures in relation to highly pathogenic avian influenza.The pet bird must be transported in a manner to prevent injury or unnecessary suffering and enter NI from GB via a Travellers Point of Entry (TPE)*.The owner or the authorised person shall, at the time of entry into NI, contact DAERA at the TPE for the purpose of documentary and identity checks..(ii) Any certification or transportation costs associated with the process are a matter between the person responsible for the Pet Bird and their official veterinarian in GB and/or their transporter. 		*Travellers Point of Entry – Designated area for the purpose of carrying out pet travel checks at NI airports and Belfast/Larne seaports.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to moving machinery and vehicles to Northern Ireland from Great Britain, to detail (i) the requirements in respect of the presence of soil and plant debris; (ii) the rationale for these requirements; and (iii) under whose remit is the (a) introduction; and (b) control of these requirements.
answer_text: (i) There are two pieces of legislation applicable to the requirement for used machinery and vehicles moving from GB to NI to be clean and free from soil. The Disease of Animals (Importation of Machinery and Vehicles) Order (NI) 2012 requires that vehicles and machinery be thoroughly scraped to remove any soil or other matter, cleansed and disinfected. Commission Implementing Regulation (CIR) (EU) 2019/2072 requires machinery and vehicles be “cleaned and free from soil and plant debris”.(ii) Soil and plant debris moving on machinery and vehicles can carry pests and pathogens with the potential to threaten our animal health, plant health and protected zone status, through the introduction of harmful organisms not currently present in Northern Ireland.  (iii) (a) CIR (EU) 2019/2072 applied in NI before EU exit and continues to apply in NI under the Windsor Framework. (iii)(b) The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is responsible for the implementation of legislative requirements when such machinery and vehicles arrive in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 7434/22-27, what steps he will take to ensure the even geographical spread of foreign investment visits, with particular regard to (i) North Antrim; and (ii) other non-Belfast constituencies.
answer_text: Regional balance in all its forms, is a key element of my department’s economic strategy. Accordingly, my department and Invest NI will actively promote regional balance across a range of economic development activities, including FDI attraction.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any Loughs Agency vehicles are fitted with tracking devices.
answer_text: Loughs Agency vehicles utilise a GPS enabled fleet management system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all categories of items which require a Northern Ireland Plant Health Label before they can be lawfully moved internally in the United Kingdom from Great Britain to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Seed potatoes moving from GB to NI must meet the Northern Ireland Plant Health Label (NIPHL) scheme conditions, which requires consignments to have a NIPHL attached.The NIPHL scheme also permits plants for planting and used agricultural and forestry machinery and vehicles to move from GB, for use only in the NI market. These consignments can also be moved from GB with Phytosanitary Certification, for use in NI or onward movement into the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 6079/17-22, whether he will address the request in the original question to detail the relevant EU laws referred to.
answer_text: Due to an administrative oversight in my Department, the annex referred to in the response to AQW 6079/17-22 was not laid in the Assembly library.  This has now been rectified and the requested list is available through the following link: http://www.niassembly.gov.uk/globalassets/documents/raise/deposited-papers/2024/dp1764.pdf.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current restrictions on vaping on public transport; and (ii) whether he has plans to bring the law into line with that prevailing on smoking.
answer_text: The policing of smoking or vaping on trains is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Battery Energy Storage Systems premises are excluded from the fire safety measures obligations, arising under Article 51(1) of the Fire and Rescue Services (Northern Ireland) Order 2006, by Article 51(2) or otherwise.
answer_text: The Fire & Rescue Service enforce the fire safety measures defined by Article 51(1); with Article 51(2) defining those outside the remit of NIFRS. It does not exclude premises from taking measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many professional operators are registered with his Department to receive goods with a NI Plant Health Label attached.
answer_text: Currently 331 NI Professional Operators are registered with DAERA to receive goods under the Northern Ireland Plant Health Label (NIPHL) scheme with a NIPHL attached.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail Translink’s policy in relation to vaping on public transport.
answer_text: Regulations regarding vaping on public transport is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the definition of relevant person in Article 52 of the Fire and Rescue Services (Northern Ireland) Order 2006 as including a person in the immediate vicinity of the premises, why do owner’s Fire Risk Assessments of Battery Energy Storage Systems and Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service audits of same not consider near neighbours of such premises.
answer_text: NIFRS do consider ‘near neighbours of such premises’ as part of the fire safety audit process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the relevant departmental guidance in respect of the operation and fire risks of Battery Energy Storage Systems; and (ii) when this departmental guidance was published.
answer_text: The Department of Health does not publish guidance in respect of the operation and fire risks of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS). However published fire safety guides can be found at https://www.nifrs.org/home/staying-safe/business-fire-safety/fire-safety-guides/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how the annual rates support, which is in the region of £350 million, will be funded.
answer_text: Rates support is funded by the Executive, and in some cases the District Councils, in the form of a loss to the gross rate income.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6763/22-27, whether he intends to return to exclusive English language branding of his Department.
answer_text: I have commissioned a review of my Department’s corporate branding and I will consider the options when this work is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following comments by the Speaker at the conclusion of Question Time on 12 March 2024, whether he will answer AQW 7959/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 7959/22-27 was answered on 22 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the seating capacity approved through planning permission for the Casement Park stadium.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any risks identified in the environmental statement of the planning process for the Casement Park project, including that of asbestos or other toxic contamination.
answer_text: Typical of many urban regeneration sites, the site is located on in-filled ground, so contamination is inevitable.The Environmental Statement identified evidence of metals in soils, asbestos containing soils, contaminants in groundwater and ground borne gases.The Planning Permission also describes the methodology for dealing with any asbestos or other contamination as part of the construction contract and demonstrates how ground treatment and removal would ensure that contamination no longer poses a risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the current position relating to previously vested Ballee lands, Ballymena; (ii) which lands have been sold back to their previous owners; (iii) whether efforts are ongoing in respect of resale to other former owners; and (iv) whether any of these lands have been disposed of to third parties.
answer_text: My Department owns lands at Ballee Road West in Ballymena that had previously been vested. None of these lands have been sold back to their previous owners or disposed of to third parties. My Department is currently considering some legal aspects of the proposed approach to disposing of these lands and will engage further with former owners and successors once the position is clarified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the answer provided to AQW 18702/17-22.
answer_text: Whilst some scoping work was initially undertaken, no further work has been progressed in the interim period due to budgetary constraints faced by my Department. The regulation of property management companies is a policy area of interest to several Departments. In the coming weeks I will engage with officials to assess how this issue can be addressed in the context of competing priorities and available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the provision of an official car to each Executive minister; and (ii) the cost to the public purse for any ministerial driver not provided by NICS or PSNI.
answer_text: The Finance Minister has been provided with an official car. The Department of Finance does not hold details of other Departments’ Ministerial cars as this is managed by each Department. The cost to the Department of Finance for a ministerial driver not provided by the NICS is the same cost as a ministerial driver provided by the NICS. The annual cost is £33,667.32 which is reflective of the 2022 NICS pay scale and is inclusive of the costs for National Insurance and Pension contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 6483-22/27, whether she will bring forward the legislation necessary to bring Article 19A of the Building Regulations (Northern Ireland) Order 1979 into operation.
answer_text: Article 19A of the 1979 Order was introduced through the Building Regulations (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2009, however, it was never commenced. While the equivalent legislation in England and Wales requiring a public register of documentation to be kept was commenced in 2006, legislation prescribing the required documentation has never been brought forward in those jurisdictions. My officials are in liaison with counterparts in England to understand why this was not brought forward and if there is an intention to do so. When that is completed, it will inform the options here for Article 19A.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps have been taken pursuant to Section 12 of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: The absence from office of a First Minister and deputy First Minister and of other Ministers for much of the period since the enactment of the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 has meant that it has not been possible to prepare and lay a report on the functioning of government.  We are aware of the statutory requirement to produce such a report and will consider further the process, content and timescales for the publication of the first report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the projected annual income from a rebuilt Casement Park; and (ii) whether any plans exist to recoup any of the cost of the project via this income.
answer_text: I am unable to provide the projected annual income from a rebuilt Casement Park as this forms part of the business case process which is ongoing. 	No plans exist to recoup any of the cost of the project via annual income, in the same way none are in place for UBIRFU or Irish FA for their projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps he is taking to ease Protocol restrictions on poultry exhibitors from Northern Ireland who wish to travel to shows in Great Britain.
answer_text: I have agreed to a meeting between by my officials and the Ulster Poultry Federation to engage in discussions regarding any challenges they may be encountering. The Windsor Framework is an international agreement between UK Government and the European Union.  Any change to the agreement or to the applicability of Animal Health Law, Official Controls Regulation and supplementing legislation in NI is a matter for the UK Government and the European Union.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps does he intend to take in relation to the availability of books on transgender and gender identity subject matters to young children through Libraries NI.
answer_text: Libraries NI has a statutory duty to provide a comprehensive and efficient public library service for persons living, working, or studying in Northern Ireland.  In line with its policy for purchasing stock, it is for Libraries NI to provide access to a wide range of quality resources to meet the needs of the entire community, providing books for a wide range of subject matter and across multiple formats.  Whilst the stocking of library books remains an operational matter, I find recent revelations to be concerning.  Libraries should be a place where parents do not need to worry about whether books their young children may pick up are age appropriate. I will be writing to the Chief Executive and Chairperson of the Library Board to request a meeting to discuss these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps she is taking to ensure police officers receive a fair and equitable pay award, in line with the process carried out in England and Wales.
answer_text: I was pleased to secure an additional allocation from the Executive in February to allow the relevant pay settlements to be taken forward.  I can confirm that I have considered and approved the recommendations for a pay award made by the Police Remuneration Review Body (PRRB). The PRRB recommendations take account of the principle of broad alignment of those made for officers in England and Wales.My officials have invited the PSNI and the Northern Ireland Policing Board to bring forward pay remits for their respective cohorts which, as with all public sector pay awards in Northern Ireland, must complete the requisite approvals processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the reported financial contribution by the government of the Republic of Ireland in relation to the Casement Park project will be paid to his Department, the GAA or elsewhere.
answer_text: The Irish government announced its intention to contribute €50 million (circa £43 million) to the project but no detail has been provided to date on how this contribution will be made and the full funding package is still to be agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) who made the decision to repurpose the £150 million of Fresh Start Agreement money for education into the transformation of public services, as part of the £3.3 billion deal; and (iii) what consultation took place with his Department in relation to this decision.
answer_text: The decision to repurpose the £150m of Fresh Start Agreement money was taken by the UK Government. There was no consultation with my department on this decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 6196/22-27, how many of these staff are employed in (i) the green or internal market lane; and (ii) the red lane.
answer_text: The staffing figure provided within my response to AQW 6196/22-27 is reflective of a multi-disciplinary flexible workforce delivering controls across all consignments in accordance with Windsor Framework requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 6196/22-27, how many of these positions would have been filled by staff before the introduction of the Protocol on Ireland/Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The figure provided within my response to AQW 6196/22-27 reflects a figure of 73 staff employed by my Department prior to 01 January 2021 and introduction of the Protocol on Ireland/Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 6196/22-27, to detail (i) the number of Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA) officials that work alongside the border staff employed by his Department; and (ii) the relationship between DEFRA officials and the border staff in relation to this work.
answer_text: Defra is responsible for the management of the existing temporary SPS inspection facilities at Northern Ireland ports and for the construction of the permanent facilities. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to support local sports people to form Northern Ireland federations, in order to allow them to compete on the international stage in the name of their own country.
answer_text: Where local sports people want to form a Northern Ireland federation, they should apply the pre-application process of the Sport NI Recognition Policy, which sets out clearly the eligibility criteria required for this process. The recognition of national governing body (NGB) for a sport is a shared process between all five Sports Councils (Sport NI along with UK Sport, Sport England, Sport Scotland and Sport Wales). The Sports Councils’ Recognition Policy 2017 can be found at the following link: http://www.sportni.net/wp-content/uploads/2019/09/2.-Recognition-Policy.pdf This process is subject to application and applications must be approved by all home country sports councils with the following exceptions:Where an NGB operates in a single home country, recognition of the body requires the approval of that country’s sports council only; andUK Sport also needs to approve applications where an Olympic or Paralympic sport or NGB is being considered.Any governing body can apply, however, under the current policy the Sports Councils will only recognise one body per sport as the lead organisation for sports that operate in Northern Ireland.Recognition of sport governing bodies is therefore an operational matter for Sport NI and the Department has no role in the recognition process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 5517/22-27, to detail his Department's continuing obligations under EU Regulation 2017/625.

These include the adoption of specific rules relating to the entry of consignments of retail goods and specific rules for plants for planting, seed potatoes, machinery and certain vehicles operated for agricultural or forestry purposes, as well as non-commercial movements of certain pet animals from Great Britain into Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6762/17-22 and AQW 6634/17-22, and given that Antrim GAA have private ownership of Casement Park and will have sole control following its completion, (i) why his Department is the contracting party in relation to its redevelopment; (ii) what legal obligations fall upon his Department in relation to its redevelopment; and (iii) whether his Department has received any indemnity from the GAA.
answer_text: I would clarify that Antrim GAA will not have private ownership and sole control of Casement Park following its completion. The Ulster Council of the GAA will own, operate and maintain the stadium upon completion, in perpetuity.Following the withdrawal of Heron Bros Ltd as main contractor for the redevelopment of Casement Park in November 2023, the Department assessed alternative procurement options for a new construction contract, with the Department acting as the contracting party instead of the UCGAA.  Following that assessment, the Department chose a UK Government Framework as the most suitable vehicle to deliver the Project within the UEFA European Championship finals 2028 timeframe.The Department is the contracting party, because the UCGAA is not a contracting authority with direct access to the relevant UK Government Framework and this approach was considered to be the most feasible for delivering the project in order to meet the timescales for the UEFA European Championship finals 2028. Once a construction contract is entered into for the delivery of the Project, the Department will owe legal obligations to the successful contractor.The existing licence (and other obligations between the UCGAA and the Department) are currently being reviewed including the indemnity elements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 7535/22-27, (i) to provide a copy of the funding agreement; (ii) to confirm that a non-public authority, such as the GAA, must comply with Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998; and (iii) to detail how the funding agreement will ensure that Casement Park is not used for any events to commemorate terrorism.
answer_text: Funding agreements entered into by the Department for Communities and other parties are commercially sensitive and exempt from disclosure.The Department requires all funding agreements to contain contractual obligations for parties to comply with sections 75 and 76 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.The funding agreement contains a number of restrictions and conditions around how the funded asset is to be used including, but not exclusively, compliance with all obligations in relation to anti-discrimination and equality legislation. These restrictions and conditions apply throughout a control period following completion of construction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 7322 22-27, (i) given that his Department provided an estimate range of costs in summer 2023, why commercial reasons prevent him from providing a current estimate of the cost of the Casement Park project; and (ii) to detail what the commercial reasons are, given that no contract has been concluded.
answer_text: I can confirm that the estimated range of costs shared through the publication of a VEAT notice in Summer 2023, was during the period actions were being taken to progress the modification of the existing construction contract. This was not a new, live procurement exercise. As the procurement for a new construction contract is in progress, there are commercial sensitivities around any discussion involving cost. Therefore, speculation around cost has the potential to prejudice discussions during this procurement process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the impact to Northern Ireland of the EU proposal to amend Council Decision 2003/17/EC; and (ii) whether any of the affected seeds from Moldova or Ukraine come in any form to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I do not expect there to be any significant impact to Northern Ireland from this EU proposal which is to grant equivalence to Moldova in respect of fodder seed and Ukraine in respect of beet, sunflower and swede rape seed. The national provisions in Moldova and Ukraine afford the same assurances on the seed’s characteristics and the arrangements for its examination, identification, marking and control as the provisions applicable to seed harvested and controlled within the European Union. The Department is not aware of any seeds from Moldova or Ukraine coming in any form to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether his Department is changing the prefix on tags for cattle and sheep; (ii) what the changes entail; and (iii) the rationale for this decision.
answer_text: The EU Animal Health Law (AHL) stipulates that Northern Ireland must use the letters XI (and the digital equivalent 899) as the prefix to all unique identification numbers assigned by my Department to livestock.  Therefore, this prefix XI will replace the UK prefix, which is currently displayed on cattle and sheep tags. My Department is finalising plans to implement this change and it is provisionally expected the transition to XI tags will be completed by January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the current budget estimate for completion of the Casement Park project; (ii) his assessment on the basis of value for money of the project; and (iii) the status of the business case.
answer_text: The current budget allocation for the Casement Park Project, approved by the Executive in 2011, is £62.5 million. The GAA have committed a contribution of £15 million and the Irish Government has announced its intention to contribute over £40 million. Any additional funding requirements will have to be agreed with partners. For commercial reasons, I cannot comment further on costs at this time.The assessment of value for money for the project will form part of the business case process.Work is progressing on completion of an updated Business Case.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) how many asylum seekers and other immigrants are presently accommodated in hotel-style accommodation; and (ii) how much financial support their Department is receiving and dispensing in support of these arrangements.
answer_text: The provision of accommodation for asylum seekers is not devolved and is the responsibility of the Home Office and its contracted housing provider Mears. Home Office publishes relevant data. The Home Office has made funding available in the last two financial years to support the dispersal of asylum seekers. The Full Dispersal fund aims to support the further development of infrastructure and enhance service provision to asylum seekers locally. Whilst it is not to support the contingency accommodation arrangements specifically, it is expected to reduce the reliance on CA by increasing dispersed accommodation more widely across the jurisdiction. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update to the answer in AQW 1329/17-22.
answer_text: You previously asked how to inspect any discharged orders that have been made by the High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland prohibiting publicity on the granting of injunctive relief. 		Following enquiries made by the NI Courts and Tribunals Service, the Lady Chief Justice’s Office has advised that five of the orders are now available for inspection.  If you wish to inspect the orders, please contact the Central Office in the Royal Courts of Justice directly via email at  centraloffice@courtsni.gov.uk.	 	In relation to the remaining two orders, the Lady Chief Justice has directed that these matters should be listed, with the legal representatives invited to appear to provide 	information to allow the court to consider the request.  I will write to you again when these matters have concluded. 		I hope this is helpful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the role of the Police Ombudsman in recommending disciplinary action while an investigation into her alleged conduct at her home in September 2023 is ongoing.
answer_text: The Ombudsman’s office is operationally independent of the Department of Justice and as these events are the subject of a live police investigation, it would be inappropriate for me to comment on them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 5643/22-27, whether his Department has access to, or means of access to, the said data and movements.
answer_text: My department does not have access to, nor the means to access the data held by the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the purpose of the £6.7 million capital funding in her Department, as referenced in Annex A of her ministerial statement of 15 February 2024.
answer_text: Unfortunately, there was an error in the table at Annex A of the Written Ministerial statement of 15 February 2024. DoF did not receive a capital allocation of £6.7 million.The allocations set out in the body of the statement were correct as was the overall total of capital allocations of £83.5 million. I have laid a revised Written Ministerial Statement to show the corrected position. Thank you for bringing this to my attention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what outcomes, since its formation, has the Foyle, Carlingford and Irish Lights Commission produced in terms of marine tourism in Lough Foyle and Carlingford Lough.
answer_text: Loughs Agency, working collaboratively with key delivery partners, has enhanced infrastructure on the Foyle which has provided the stimulus for international regattas and festivals that have attracted thousands of visitors, yielded substantial economic benefits and enhanced the region’s reputation as a marine tourism destination.Loughs Agency has also engaged extensively with local communities and businesses in both regions to promote innovation and skills development, securing external funding that has helped to produced marine tourism ventures such as a surf school and a water based adventure centre. Loughs Agency has also improved access to training and safety equipment in the Carlingford area, enabling local marine tourism businesses to grow and develop in the region.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the projected costs of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act) was brought through Westminster by the Northern Ireland Office.TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Act for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies, namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.This will be in line with our decisions.Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to publish (i) a list of Special Advisers appointed across the Executive; and (ii) the salary bands of each Special Adviser.
answer_text: The process for the appointment of special advisers is underway. Once this process is complete, details of Special Advisers will be published, including salary bands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what outcomes, since its formation, has the Foyle, Carlingford and Irish Lights Commission produced in terms of development and licensing of aquaculture in the Foyle and Carlingford areas.
answer_text: While the Foyle and Carlingford Fisheries (Northern Ireland) Order 2007 extended the functions of the Foyle, Carlingford and Irish Lights Commission (FCILC) to include the development and licensing of aquaculture in the Foyle and Carlingford areas, the provisions of that Order have not all been commenced.  Therefore, it is my Department which is currently responsible for the development and licensing of aquaculture in Northern Ireland.		The FCILC has, however, been proactive in conducting work in preparation for the transfer of aquaculture development and licensing.  The work to date includes: the development of an Aquaculture and Shellfisheries Management Strategy; a Strategic Environmental Assessment of that Strategy; and, in collaboration with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute and other operational partners, the development of ecological carrying capacity models for shellfish in Foyle and Carlingford Loughs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether the prefix on tags for sheep will change; (ii) what this change entails; (iii) the reasons for this change; and (iv) when the change will become operative.
answer_text: The EU Animal Health Law (AHL) stipulates that Northern Ireland must use the letters XI (and the digital equivalent 899) as the prefix to all unique identification numbers assigned by my Department to livestock, including sheep.  Therefore, this prefix XI will replace the UK prefix which is currently displayed on sheep tags. My Department is finalising plans to implement this change and it is provisionally expected the transition to XI tags will be completed by January 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether the office accommodation built by the Loughs Agency in Omagh was completed within budget; (ii) the budgeted cost; and (iii) the actual cost.
answer_text: Construction of the Loughs Agency’s office accommodation in Omagh commenced in September 2019 but was subsequently stopped in March 2020 due to the Covid 19 pandemic. Consequently, the delay in completing construction, along with the increased cost of labour and materials, meant that construction was not completed within time or budget. The budgeted cost for this project was £460,000 (excluding VAT and professional fees) and the actual cost of the project, co-funded by my Department and the Department of the Environment, Climate & Communications in Ireland was £803,000 (including VAT & professional fees).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will commission an examination of the Policing Board process that approved the secondment of the Deputy Chief Constable.
answer_text: The process around the approval of the secondment of the Deputy Chief Constable is properly a matter for the Northern Ireland Policing Board.It would be inappropriate for me to comment on the process as it is a Human Resources issue between the Northern Ireland Policing Board and the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why the Loughs Agency is paying for renovations of the office it rents in Carlingford.
answer_text: The Loughs Agency previously leased premises (across two different sites in Carlingford) which in 2020 were condemned as not-fit-for purpose. An Economic Appraisal considered a number of alternative options and the option to lease premises on Carlingford Lough that required renovation demonstrated the best value for money, whilst meeting all the needs of the Agency. Options to buy or lease commercial premises were extremely limited, and this was the only viable option to secure premises that would meet the needs of the Agency in the required location.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the cost of the office extension work carried out for the Loughs Agency in Londonderry; and (ii) how the contract was awarded.
answer_text: The overall cost of the project to enhance Loughs Agency’s premises in Derry / Londonderry was £155,000 plus VAT. The work undertaken was aimed at optimising the utilisation of available space and was not an extension of the premises. The contract for this work was awarded through the 'eTenders' portal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the current staff complement of the Loughs Agency; (ii) how many work in (a) Northern Ireland; and (b) Republic of Ireland; (iii) the current budget of the Loughs Agency; and (iv) the office accommodation the Loughs Agency occupies.
answer_text: The staff complement of Loughs Agency is 53 although it currently employs 59 people, including 12 staff on temporary or fixed term contracts. Although Loughs Agency employees work across both jurisdictions, 51 are headquartered in Northern Ireland and 8 are headquartered in Republic of Ireland, split across three office locations in Derry / Londonderry, Omagh and Carlingford.		The Loughs Agency’s agreed budget for 2023 was £6,588,000, co-funded by my Department and the Department of the Environment, Climate & Communications in the Republic of Ireland. The 2024 business plan, which incorporates their budget, has not yet been considered by the North South Ministerial Council.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the decision by Heron Bros Limited, (i) how the procurement process will operate in respect of the Casement Park project; and (ii) who will supervise this process.
answer_text: Work is progressing on the procurement of a new contractor for the main construction works utilising a UK Government Framework for major construction projects. My Department is responsible for this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his comments in the Assembly on 12 February 2024 mean he has rejected the advice of the Independent Panel on Education in regards to the Strule Campus.
answer_text: I do not intend to end the Strule Programme, as recommended by the Review. My officials are finalising work on the Full Business Case for the Campus which will set out the benefits and costs of the Programme in full. I have asked the Executive to recommit £150m of former Fresh Start funding for the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the current funding provision for long COVID sufferers will be continued; and (ii) what his long-term plans are to deal adequately with the needs of sufferers of long COVID.
answer_text: My Department’s planning assumption is that the same level of funding that was provided in 2023/24 will be available in the next financial year.  However, this is subject to budget availability which is yet to be confirmed.My Department is working with the PHA on a review of long COVID services.  This includes an assessment of the ongoing demand for services; a review of current service provision; and, taking cognisance of service developments elsewhere in the UK. This will inform the service model moving forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the process that will now be followed in respect of the Casement Park project; (ii) , in terms of oversight, whether his Department is now the contracting party; and (iii) to detail his Department’s funding commitments on the project.
answer_text: Work is progressing on the procurement of a new contractor for the main construction works utilising a UK Government Framework for major construction projects. My Department is the contracting authority for these procurement arrangements.  The Executive has committed £62.5 million towards the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to return to English language branding of his Department.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has not moved away from English language branding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether anyone, other than the GAA, will have any residual interest, including profits from commercial use, on completion of the Casement Park project.
answer_text: The commercial management and operation of Casement Park will be the sole responsibility of the GAA on completion of the Casement Park project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the funding model for Community Pharmacy Services.
answer_text: I very much appreciate the extremely important work that community pharmacies deliver for our population. I have received representation from pharmacy contractors and from their representative body, Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI). An urgent meeting was held this week with representatives of CPNI to consider the funding issues. I have asked my Department to address some of the pressures that pharmacies are facing within what is available and affordable. I encouraged CPNI to continue to work with Department officials to seek ways to ensure the pharmacy service can develop in line with our shared strategic objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has ordered the withdrawal of the warrant cards of Trading Standards officers and their reissuing in bilingual format.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) what value for money assessment has been made on his decision to rebrand his Department on a bilingual basis; and (ii) to detail the anticipated cost.
answer_text: The small sum required to rebrand the Department on a bilingual basis involved commensurate effort in a value for money assessment.The anticipated cost for the rebrand work is estimated at £5,270. No additional costs are anticipated as any future updates will be part of normal business activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the processes and procedures required of a Northern Ireland fishing boat, fishing in UK waters, when it comes to landing its catch at a Northern Ireland port; (ii) the legal basis for these processes and procedures; and (iii) how this differs if the catch is landed in a GB port.
answer_text: There are a number of requirements that need to be met by Northern Ireland fishing boats landing their catches at Northern Ireland Ports. Masters of Northern Ireland fishing vessels:of 12 metres length or more must notify DAERA Sea fisheries Inspectorate at least 4 hours before their estimated time of landing with information relating to the fishing trip, including the name of the port of destination, submission of an electronic logbook listing quantities of each species to be landed, dates of fishing operations, fishing gear used, and the area where fishing activity was conducted. A landing declaration must be submitted electronically within 24 hours of landing;Vessels between 10 metres and 12 metres in length must submit a paper logbook and landing declaration to DAERA Sea fisheries Inspectorate as soon as possible and not later than 48 hours after landing; andVessels of 10 metres in length and under provide paper catch records to the Sea Fisheries Inspectorate on a monthly basis.   The legal basis for these procedures and processes are provided by UK law, specifically retained Regulation (EC) 1224/2009 establishing a Community control system for ensuring compliance with the rules of the common fisheries policy.However, the EU version of Regulation (EC) 1224/2009, insofar as it concerns provisions relating to marketing standards, which includes the weighing of fish upon landing as a requirement for the completion of a landing declaration, continues to apply in Northern Ireland by being listed in Annex II of the Windsor Framework.Although the legal basis is slightly different for Northern Ireland fishing vessels landing into Northern Ireland, compared to a port in Great Britain, in that EU law applies for marketing standards, there is no practical difference in the processes and procedures for a Northern Ireland fishing vessel landing its catch into either a port in Northern Ireland or Great Britain.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans has he to revise the statutory guidance for schools on Relationships and Sexuality Education.
answer_text: In June 2023, the Secretary of State of Northern Ireland, made and laid Regulations under his powers in section 9 of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2019.  The Regulations make changes to the Northern Ireland curriculum to add teaching about sexual and reproductive health and rights covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion as part of Learning for Life and Work at key stages 3 and 4.The Regulations also require the Department to issue guidance by 1 January 2024 to ensure that:pupils receive education on sexual and reproductive health and rights, covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion; andthe education received by pupils is age-appropriate, comprehensive and scientifically accurate.My Department therefore issued guidance to schools on 1 January 2024 on the changes to the Education (Curriculum Minimum Content) Order (Northern Ireland) 2007 (“the 2007 Order”) made by the Secretary of State. This specifically covers age-appropriate and scientifically accurate education on sexual and reproductive health and rights covering prevention of early pregnancy and access to abortion in the curriculum.  In line with the 2007 Order, the guidance does not prescribe the detailed content of what that schools are to teach.  The Board of Governors and the Principal have statutory responsibility to deliver the minimum curriculum content but they also have autonomy over when, how and who is involved in curriculum delivery, aligned with the ethos of the school.The guidance also sets out the circumstances in which, at the request of a parent, a pupil may be excused from the new elements of the Learning for Life and Work curriculum introduced by the Secretary of State.In April 2023, the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) published a thematic evaluation of the preventative curriculum (of which RSE is a part).  The Department has established a task and finish group that is considering currently how best to respond to the ETI findings.  Further guidance on RSE in schools will be considered by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to Article 19A of the Buildings Regulations (NI) Order 1979, (i) whether local councils are required to keep a public register of documentation arising in connection with building regulations; (ii) whether the required documentation has been prescribed by her Department.
answer_text: The legislation required to bring Article 19A of the Building Regulations NI Order 1979 (Registers to be kept by district councils) into operation has not been brought forward. There is therefore no requirement on local councils to keep a register of information or documentation in connection with building regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the drug tariff payable to community pharmacies.
answer_text: My Department undertook a consultation seeking to improve the Drug Tariff arrangements in April 2022. Work is currently in train to take forward improvements. In light of representations from the sector, an urgent meeting has been arranged this week to meet with representatives of CPNI and part of that agenda will be consideration of further changes to the Drug Tariff

answer_text: The total funding from his Department to the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the report on the Non-Domestic Renewable Heat Incentive Scheme consultation will be published.
answer_text: A comprehensive report of the consultation, indicating how responses were considered in the formulation of policy, will be published following a decision by the Executive on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the process followed by the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice when a grievance is raised by staff.
answer_text: The NI Hospice is a registered charity and is not an HSC Employer.  It is therefore for the hospice to consider and determine the terms and conditions for its staff and develop appropriate guidance for any grievance process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Children's Hospice pay bands and pay scales fail to relate to those employed by the Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: AfC pay arrangements apply to staff directly employed in the HSC under Agenda for Change (AfC) terms and conditions of service, for whom the Department has direct responsibility for rates of pay.The NI Hospice is a registered charity and is not an HSC Employer.  It is therefore for the hospice to consider and determine the terms and conditions for its staff, having considered its overall financial position and funding available to it.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission who gave permission for the Taoiseach to hold a press conference in the Great Hall on 5 February 2024, having regard to the Assembly Media Handbook.
answer_text: Visiting dignitaries, including Prime Ministers and Taoisigh, regularly visit Parliament Buildings on official business. As has been practice since the establishment of the Northern Ireland Assembly, visiting dignitaries have had the opportunity to reflect on their visits when interviewed by members of the media in the Great Hall. Use of the Great Hall for such visits, which attract significant media attention, provides a weather-proofed and well-lit area for media guests. This long-held practice is in line with the Assembly Commission’s Media Handbook.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) who gave the direction that the main staircase in the Great Hall was not accessible to MLAs while the Taoiseach gave a press conference on 5 February 2024; and (ii) to detail the basis on which this decision was taken.
answer_text: Assembly Commission officials make operational decisions in order to maintain free flow of people in Parliament Buildings at busy times, particularly when large groups of people are present in public areas such as the Great Hall. This was the case on 5 February 2024, when a decision was taken in real time to ensure passage ways and access to the stairs in the Great Hall remained free. This was done to facilitate movement and building users were directed to other access routes for a short period of time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many border staff are employed by his Department; and (ii) how many posts his Department still intends to fill.
answer_text: My Department does not employ border staff but is required by law to ensure a sufficient number of suitably qualified and experienced staff are available to perform official controls and other official activities efficiently and effectively at SPS Inspection Facilities.Currently my Department employs 151.5 staff, across a range of disciplines and expertise, in support of delivering official controls and activities at SPS Inspection Facilities, and intends to fill 28 vacancies deemed necessary to ensure it continues to meet its statutory obligations in this regard.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps their Department has taken, or plans to take, on foot of Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: We note that the UK Government has, in its Command Paper, committed to establishing a new working group with the Northern Ireland Executive, convened with the Executive Office, to consider the operation of the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the First Minister’s visit to the Palestinian Mission to the UK on 8 February 2024 was publicly funded.
answer_text: It was not.
