question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) each EU law referenced in Annex 2 of the Northern Ireland Protocol which impacts the work of his Department; (ii) each such EU law adopted since the publication of Annex 2 which impacts the work of his Department; and (iii) each EU law which has been removed from application in respect of his Department and its responsibilities.
answer_text: No EU law referenced in Annex 2 of the Northern Ireland Protocol or adopted since the publication of Annex 2 directly impacts the work of the Department of Education.The Department of Education is not directly impacted by specific EU laws and does not retain a list of removed EU laws.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what guidance is provided to Executive Office Ministers to ensure they only represent agreed Executive positions when undertaking publicly funded foreign visits.
To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he will halt the redevelopment of Casement Park; and (ii) to provide justification to any continuance to this project.
answer_text: Casement Park is part of the Regional Stadia Programme, which is an Executive commitment of more than a decade. The Executive has committed £62.5 Million towards the redevelopment, however further funding will be required for this to progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many abortions have been performed since 15 November 2021.
answer_text: The Department is unable to provide the information requested from the date of 15 November 2021.  However, since the introduction of the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No 2) Regulations 2020 on 31 March 2020, there have been a total of 7,681 abortions carried out in Northern Ireland to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many border posts, arising from the NI Protocol, have been established; and (ii) how their presence and operation is affected by Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union, or any related statutory instruments.
answer_text: In terms of response, my preference would be below.Sanitary and Phytosanitary (SPS) inspection facilities are in place at Belfast, Larne, Warrenpoint and Foyle Ports and Belfast International Airport.  Inspection facilities existed at Belfast Port, Larne Port and Belfast International Airport pre EU Exit.  These facilities are necessary to enable the Department, as competent authority, to fulfil its statutory responsibilities including as set out in the Official Controls Regulations.I refer the member to AQW 5639/22-27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there is a legislative or practical impact on her Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: At this juncture, officials have not identified any legislative or practical impact on my Department as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union. My Department will, however, continue to monitor in advance of any anticipated developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there is a legislative or practical impact on his Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: My officials are currently assessing the implications of the Command Paper.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will make representations to the Education Authority to withdraw its Guidance for Schools, EOTAS Centre and Youth Service on Supporting Transgender Young People.
answer_text: My officials are considering the current guidance in light of emerging practice and potential legislative changes elsewhere. I have asked that this matter be concluded expeditiously and following that I will determine an appropriate course of action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there is a legislative or practical impact on her Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: I note the new democratic scrutiny aspects referred to in the paper. My officials will support the new Committee in their consideration of legislation in scope, relevant to my Department.     I also note the reference to bolstering east-west transport links and my officials will consider this  as part of improving connectivity across these islands when developing my Department’s draft transport strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a legislative or practical impact on his Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: Early assessment of the Command Paper indicates that there may be some practical and legislative changes that could impact on my Department, including utilisation of the UK internal market system for medical supplies remaining in Northern Ireland.I note the establishment of the Democratic Scrutiny Committee and my officials will support their work.  The practical impact will not be known until requests are received by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether there is a legislative or practical impact on her Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: The command paper does not itself have any legislative or practical impact on my Department. My officials will monitor the British Government’s implementation of the Command Paper to assess any impact within the responsibilities of the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there is a legislative or practical impact on his Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: The Command Paper, “Safeguarding the Union” primarily addresses issues that arise out of the Windsor Framework. As the Windsor Framework did not relate to functions exercised by my Department it is not anticipated that there will be any legislative impact by its provisions. However Annex B of the Command Paper, “Strengthening Northern Ireland’s place in the Union” sets out measures relevant to my Department, such as a twinning programme between schools in Northern Ireland and Great Britain, to enable the full diversity of our Union to be experienced by a wider range of young people building shared connections and demonstrating the value of Northern Ireland’s integral place in the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there is a legislative or practical impact on his Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: The Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union, has no immediate legal effect.Any queries arising from the Command Paper and the discharge of any commitments within it are a matter for the UK Government.  As the Member will be aware, two pieces of legislation specific to the Command Paper, namely The Windsor Framework (Constitutional Status of Northern Ireland) Regulations 2024 and The Windsor Framework (UK Internal Market and Unfettered Access) Regulations 2024, have been laid by the UK Government and are making their way through Parliament in the usual manner. Any further legislative amendments are a matter for the UK Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there is a legislative or practical impact on his Department and its responsibilities as a result of the Command Paper, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: My officials will monitor the implementation of the UK Government's Command Paper 'Safeguarding the Union', consider the legislative or practical impacts for this Department and take forward its responsibilities.Annex B of the Paper makes specific reference to promoting Ulster Scots heritage and supporting sporting and cultural Links, including delivery of the Sub Regional Football Stadia Strategy and my officials are content that the Department is in a position to fulfil these responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the Government’s commitment that more than 80 per cent of all freight movements from Great Britain to Northern Ireland will take place under the UK internal market system, as outlined in Command Paper 1021 Safeguarding the Union; and (ii) what mechanism is available to him to measure this.
answer_text: Operation of the lane, and therefore data and movements, are the responsibility of the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in light of Rooney and Ors in a matter for Judicial Review in the matter of the decision made by the Minister for DAERA on 2 February 2022 and Command Paper 1021 Safeguarding the Union, to outline the continuing obligations under EU Regulation 2017/625.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework, EU Regulation 2017/625 (Official Controls Regulation (OCR)) continues to apply in Northern Ireland. However, recognising Northern Ireland’s unique situation, the Windsor Framework Agreement, and subsequent EU Regulation 2023/1231 (SPS Regulation), introduces derogation from specific provisions within OCR. These include the adoption of specific rules relating to the entry of consignments of retail goods and specific rules for plants for planting, seed potatoes, machinery and certain vehicles operated for agricultural or forestry purposes, as well as non-commercial movements of certain pet animals from Great Britain into Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) what is the Executive's agreed position on checks on goods at ports; and (ii) whether the existing guidance or direction has changed.
answer_text: ANSWER Guidance on the checks of goods is not a matter for the Executive Office.  The issue has not been considered by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the Casement Park project; (ii) what steps he intends to take as a result of that assessment; and (iii) what departmental funding is anticipated as being required for the project.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Casement Park is a flagship project for the Executive.Since taking up office, I have been provided with a high-level overview of the project from senior officials. Further to this, I have requested a detailed briefing on all aspects of the project. The current allocation of public funding to the Casement Park Project, approved by the Executive in 2011, is £62.5 million. Any additional funding requirements will have to be agreed with partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans exist to dismantle the border posts at ports, following the publication of the Command Paper 1021, Safeguarding the Union.
answer_text: There are no plans to dismantle the current Sanitary and Phytosanitary (SPS) inspection facilities at Northern Ireland ports, as they are required to facilitate necessary checks on SPS goods moving into Northern Ireland from Third Countries as set out in the Official Controls Regulation and as explained in the Command Paper 1021, Safeguarding the Union.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what checks and paperwork are required on goods moving through the UK Internal Market Scheme.
answer_text: The UK Internal Market Scheme relates to customs and duty.  The operation of the scheme is therefore a matter for His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will request a portrait of His Majesty the King for display in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has not discussed whether it will request a portrait of His Majesty the King for display in Parliament Buildings.The Assembly Commission completed a review of the items on display in Parliament Buildings in 2022.  This resulted in the agreement of an inclusive package of items and images related to the parliamentary history of Parliament Buildings which was installed and unveiled as a permanent exhibition.  The exhibition included two photographs of Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II on visits to the Stormont Estate.Any proposal to include a new portrait to the items and images on display would require agreement by the Assembly Commission to add to the overall existing package, including the potential for other proposals to be made.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5409/22-27, to outline (i) the number of rooms that are not in use on the fourth floor in Parliament Buildings; and (ii) the reasons why the rooms are not in use.
answer_text: Further to the response in AQW 5409/22-22, the Assembly Commission can confirm that all rooms on the fourth floor are in use.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission how many rooms in Parliament Buildings are out of commission as a result of water ingress and associated problems.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is aware of problems with damp ingress related to the roof project and all affected areas are being kept under regular review.Recent spells of heavy rainfall have resulted in water ingress in some areas of the 3rd and 4th floor, however there are no rooms in Parliament Buildings out of commission as a result of water ingress and associated problems.   In respect of issues related to the roof project, the Assembly Commission has issued proceedings against two parties for negligence, breach of statutory duty and breach of contract. Proposals have been submitted for further investigatory works to take place to identify the problem(s) and enable repairs to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the water ingress into Parliament Buildings, including where the ingress is occurring.
answer_text: Water ingress on the 3rd floor corridor and in some 4th floor offices is still being investigated as part of the ongoing legal proceedings in respect of issues related to the roof of Parliament Buildings.Any new incidences of water ingress are investigated as they are identified. Any that may be related to the roof project will be dealt with through the ongoing legal proceedings, otherwise they are addressed separately by the Assembly Commission.A number of the more recent minor water ingress incidents that have occurred and their status are noted below.A leak from a radiator into the ceiling opposite Room 383 has been repaired.Water ingress around the doors onto the roof from the 3rd floor west staircase where the damage to the ceiling below was becoming more evident is due to be fitted with a new threshold in October 2023. A hole in the flashing on the roof is due to be repaired in October 2023.Water ingress at the base of the west door on the roof, has been repaired with a new threshold.A leak from the main water tanks (or associated pipework) in the tank room at Parliament Buildings has been resolved.A water leak that was staining the ceiling in the Long Gallery has been investigated and sealed.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the cost of the work undertaken in the Assembly Chamber over recent months.
answer_text: The last substantial updates to the video and audio systems in the Assembly Chamber were in 2008 and 1998. When the Assembly Commission considered this matter in 2021 the broadcasting equipment in the Assembly Chamber was at the end of its useful life, some of the audio equipment was over 30 years old, and the number of broadcasting faults and failures was increasing.Given the outdated nature of the existing broadcasting infrastructure in the Assembly Chamber, coupled with the significant risk of failure in delivering essential broadcasting services to the Assembly, it became imperative to initiate a robust upgrade programme. This programme was approved by the Assembly Commission in November 2021 and was split into two phases.  It commenced in the summer of 2022 with upgrading the video systems and associated works, with the second phase in the summer of 2023 involving upgrading of the audio systems and associated works. As a result of this programme of work the Assembly now has resilient, contemporary, efficient, reliable, and flexible systems to meet both current and future broadcasting requirements.The breakdown of the elements involved in this project is as follows:Assembly Chamber VideoInstallation of broadcasting standard video facilities, including cameras, robotics, and control room facilities.Graphics systems to enhance the visual representation of information.Chamber display screens (Public Gallery) for better visibility and information dissemination.Technical Furniture (Basement) to house the new technology seamlessly.Assembly Chamber AudioInstallation of broadcasting standard digital sound systems, incorporating modern microphones, loudspeakers, and digital processing units.Network switches for audio over IP, enhancing connectivity and flexibility.Technical furniture (Chamber and Basement) to house the new technology seamlessly.Central Equipment and Control RoomsUpgrading core facilities to modern standards.Talkback system and stations to facilitate communication across the broadcasting control rooms and galleries.Equipment cabinets and management server computers for efficient storage and management of the new systems.Cabling and termination, including all local and long-distance cabling for specialist video and sound.Integration with existing equipment, to ensure seamless transition and functionality with existing infrastructure.GeneralDetailed technical designs, including final equipment layout and connections.Testing and commissioning, to guarantee the reliability and efficiency of the new systems.Training on the management and operation of the new systems.The cost to date for this works carried out in 2022 and 2023 is £1.53m.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission when did the Assembly decide that facilities for electronic voting should be installed in the Assembly Chamber.
answer_text: The Assembly has not decided to install an electronic voting system and one has not been installed.In 2021, in response to correspondence from the Speaker, the Committee on Procedures commissioned a research paper on electronic voting. The Committee’s 2022 Legacy Report identified that establishing the scale and scope of any potential preferred system for electronic voting at the Assembly was a significant piece of work which it would not be able to complete in the time remaining in the mandate and said that this issue should be added to the potential topics for consideration by a successor Committee. In November 2021 the Assembly Commission approved a business case to upgrade the audio and video systems in the Assembly Chamber, which included consideration of inputs (e.g. cabling and panels) to potentially facilitate electronic voting. During 2022 and 2023 the Assembly Commission implemented an extensive programme of work to upgrade the audio and video systems in the Assembly Chamber, together with associated works. This programme of work did not include the installation of an electronic voting system, however panels and cabling have been provided that would facilitate the implementation of electronic voting if a decision to implement such a system is taken in the future by the Assembly. The scope of any electronic voting system will be a matter for the next Assembly Committee on Procedures to consider and for the Assembly to agree.While electronic voting systems are not currently a feature in the Assembly Chamber, the most recent work has provided an element of future proofing for the needs of the Assembly in the years ahead.  This should ensure a smoother transition, should a decision be taken to introduce electronic voting, by reducing the level of infrastructure work required and the associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 2832/22-27, for an update on the status of the legal proceedings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has issued proceedings against Hamilton Architects LLP and Tracey Brothers Ltd for negligence, breach of statutory duty and breach of contract as a consequence of defects in certain works, as set out in AQW 2832/22-27.The parties are presently exchanging interlocutory correspondence. This may allow a narrowing of the issues in dispute in this case prior to the Assembly Commission seeking to set it down for trial.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether those MLAs, who held a ministerial office until 27 October 2022, continue to have access to their ministerial room in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: Those MLAs who held ministerial office do not have direct access to their previously held ministerial offices. A small number of keys for the ministerial offices are currently held by civil servant staff and these will be retrieved by the Usher Services team.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5368/22-27, on the basis that the money taken in during 2023 is of the order of that taken in during 2022, what is the anticipated subsidy that the Assembly will pay in 2023 in relation to the catering element of the contract.
answer_text: For the first year of the contract (9 January 2023 to 8 January 2024), it is anticipated that the operating cost of the catering element of the contract is £274k.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5368/22-27, in relation to the catering element of the contract, how often does the Assembly make a reconciliation between the monies taken in and the cost of the services provided and pay a subsidy to the contractor.
answer_text: The reconciliation between the monies taken in and the contractually agreed return on sales forms part of the monthly account documentation submitted by the contractor. This provides back-up to the monthly invoice for the provision of all services included under this contract.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5368/22-27, to detail (i) the amount of money taken in during 2022 in respect of the services to which the contract applies; and (ii) the resulting subsidy paid by the Assembly in 2022.
answer_text: The amount of money taken in during 2022 was £489k from all catering outlets. The operating cost of the catering element of the contract for 2022 was £311k.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission why do problems persist with internet connectivity in Members’ offices in Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: Due in part to the greater demand for video conferencing by Members and staff, Information Systems (IS) Office has identified deficiencies in the current Wi-Fi service, both in terms of signal availability in certain areas of Parliament Buildings and in the reliability of Wi-Fi connections to sustain high bandwidth applications. IS Office accepts that the system cannot cope reliably with these increased demands.The Assembly Commission has agreed to replace the Wi-Fi network infrastructure, which will include a dedicated internet connection. This project to replace the Wi-Fi network has now commenced and the target date for completion of works is 30 June 2023.Pending the full replacement of the Wi-Fi network, the IS Office has reissued written advice to Members on how best to manage Wi-Fi connections on mobile devices when in Parliament Buildings. Should Members experience any difficulty with Wi-Fi access or performance in Parliament Buildings they should contact the IS Office Service Desk at the relevant time. The IS Office will continue to monitor the current Wi-Fi service in Parliament Buildings and will respond appropriately to any specific issues as they arise.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) on what basis Mr Pat Sheehan MLA has been attending and participating in Assembly Commission meetings; (ii) whether his participation includes voting rights; (iii) when and how he was appointed to the Assembly Commission; and (iv) how is his participation consistent with statutory provisions and Standing Orders.
answer_text: Pat Sheehan MLA is attending meetings of the Assembly Commission in line with a previously agreed Assembly Commission procedure which is fully compliant with statutory provisions and Standing Orders.  Mr Sheehan has not been appointed to the Assembly Commission and cannot vote at Assembly Commission meetings.  It has been the practice throughout the lifetime of the Assembly that a representative from each of the five main parties has been appointed to the Assembly Commission.  This is to ensure that the Assembly Commission considers the views of the vast majority of the Assembly when making decisions.In May 2019, the Assembly Commission faced the situation of a vacancy arising in the membership of the Assembly Commission at a time when the Assembly was not sitting, so the vacancy could not be filled.  	The Assembly Commission therefore agreed an interim protocol to allow attendance at Assembly Commission meetings by the party affected by the vacancy.	The interim protocol allows a nominated Member of the party affected by the vacancy to attend meetings of the Assembly Commission to reflect that party’s views.The interim protocol specifies that the Member nominated to attend is not a member of the Assembly Commission and sets out the restrictions on his/ her role.  For instance, that the nominated Member cannot vote, cannot answer Assembly Written Questions or chair a meeting of the Assembly Commission in the absence of the Speaker.Following the resignation of Alex Attwood, in June 2019, under the terms of the interim protocol, Colin McGrath MLA was nominated to attend meetings of the Assembly Commission.  In June 2022, following the appointment of John O’Dowd MLA as a Minister, the Assembly Commission agreed to again follow the previous convention and invite Sinn Féin to nominate a Member to attend meetings of the Assembly Commission until the existing vacancy could be filled.Sinn Féin nominated Pat Sheehan MLA to attend meetings of the Assembly Commission.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether any MLA can attend meetings of the Assembly Commission.
answer_text: No, attendance at the Assembly Commission meetings is only by invitation from the Assembly Commission.  It has been longstanding practice that only Members appointed to the Assembly Commission or Members nominated under the terms of the interim protocol when vacancies cannot be filled, have attended meetings of the Assembly Commission.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what is the level of annual subsidy provided for, or anticipated, in respect of the new catering contract.
answer_text: A new Support Services contract providing catering, cleaning, non-catering porterage and ad-hoc services commenced at Parliament Buildings on Monday 9 January 2023. As part of the contract, the Assembly Commission requires that catering services are provided during business hours and for events / functions. Where the cost of providing these services exceeds the monies taken in, the extra cost is incurred by the Assembly. For the first year of the contract (9 January 2023 to 8 January 2024), it is anticipated that the operating cost of the contract will be £632k. The anticipated operating cost of the catering element of the contract is £274k.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) whether there are permanent or time limited contracts for the Irish/English interpretation service; (ii) whether the service is provided and paid on a usage basis; (iii) whether retainer fees are paid for the service; and (iv) what the cost of the service has been to date.
answer_text: In the Office of the Official Report (Hansard):Three members of staff support the Assembly’s direction to the Assembly Commission to provide simultaneous and passive interpretation of Assembly business in Irish, where there is appropriate demand. One member of Assembly Commission staff (permanently employed) and two members of temporary agency staff support the delivery of this service.The service is not provided or paid for on a usage basis.Retainer fees are not paid for the service.The total cost to the Assembly Commission for the permanent member of staff, up to 31 December 2022, was £39,243.11. The total cost to the Assembly Commission for the agency staff, up to 13 January 2023, was £77,832.30.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5360/22-27, by how much it is being recompensed for the services provided by its staff to other entities.
answer_text: As advised in the response to AWQ 5360/22-27, staff who ordinarily provide direct support to Members can provide assistance to other entities including other legislatures at this time. The Assembly Commission has not sought recompense for this work. It is recognised that this work provides staff with the opportunity to maintain their skill base and further their own learning.  From time to time, the Assembly Commission may facilitate formal temporary secondment agreements for its staff and such arrangements attract the recovery of salary costs. There are no staff currently on formal secondment to another body, to which salary recovery would apply.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what was the cost of (i) acquiring the new artefacts and material for the permanent displays in Parliament Buildings; and (ii) the work taken to put these displays in place.
answer_text: On 17 February 2021, the Assembly Commission agreed the principles and terms of reference to guide a review of the display of artefacts in Parliament Buildings.   The new collection of approximately 100 images and items, comprises: items owned by the Assembly Commission which were previously on display;those owned by the Assembly Commission which were previously in storage or non-public areas; a range of images not previously displayed in the building; and, storyboards providing visitors with context and background to the items displayed.During the 13 months between the Assembly Commission’s decision and the launch of the new display on 23 March 2022, a significant amount of work was required to deliver the project.  However, significant savings were made on the potential cost of the exhibition by Assembly Commission officials undertaking the work on the development of the final proposals, the gathering of images, the drafting of accompanying text and overseeing the installation.  In addition to costs for permissions to reproduce images, printing and framing, the only external assistance to the project was provided on a consultancy basis by Dr Éamon Phoenix to ensure the project had access to the necessary historical expertise. question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission how many Secretariat staff are on secondment to other bodies during the present interregnum affecting the Assembly.
answer_text: At present, there are no Assembly Commission staff on formal secondment to other bodies.While Plenary and Committee meetings are not currently taking place, all other activities and services that take place in Parliament Buildings are continuing as normal. This includes services in relation to education, Assembly outreach, communications, finance and human resources. It is therefore necessary that Assembly Commission employees are retained to undertake this work and to ensure that readiness is maintained for a return to full Assembly business.However, it is also recognised that staff who ordinarily provide direct support to Members can provide assistance to other entities at this time.  A number of business areas are therefore providing support to other legislatures.  This will enable staff to further their own learning and skills base and also seek to ensure value for money. In addition, some staff have been redeployed to other areas to support the completion of specific work projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) why the retirement age for prison officers is out of line with other services such as the police and fire services; and (ii) what consideration is being given to parity.
answer_text: (i) The Northern Ireland Assembly considered pension age during the passage of the Public Service Pensions Act (NI) 2014. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many specialised staff in bereavement care are available in each maternity hospital in Northern Ireland; and (ii) what funding is available to sustain adequate bereavement care.
answer_text: All healthcare staff working in maternity and neonatal services in Northern Ireland follow the Department of Health’s Regional Bereavement Guidance on evidence-based, holistic care of parents and their families after the experience of miscarriage, stillbirth or neonatal death. This guidance establishes a regional standard of care throughout Northern Ireland. Pathways are in place for the emotional and practical support of parents who experience miscarriage, stillbirth or the death of a baby shortly after birth.  The team involved in providing care to the bereaved woman will work with her and the family to navigate the legal, procedural and emotional challenges following the death of a baby.  In addition, each Trust has a bereavement midwife resource to provide additional support to parents and families who have lost a baby through miscarriage, stillbirth or where their baby has died shortly after birth. Bereavement midwives also offer support and training to other members of staff to ensure that they can provide support to parents and families in these challenging circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether there is a difference in the approach to providing an uplift in assistance, in the current cost of living crisis, between nursing and social work students; and (ii) whether there are plans to help social work students with travelling costs which arise directly from their work experience duties in the community.
answer_text: The schemes in place to support student nurses and student social workers were developed separately, with variations in thresholds and eligibility. The Social Work Student Incentive Scheme is intended for all NI Degree in Social Work students who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland. Social Work students can access a non-means tested payment of £4,000 for each year of their study.  In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements.  The DOH Student Incentive Scheme is in addition to any student finance support, which can be applied for separately. I recognise the additional hardship faced by students undertaking clinical courses due to the cost of living crisis. Unfortunately, due to budgetary constraints, my Department is unable to provide more assistance to students than it does currently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what measures have been taken by the Education Authority to ensure that the issues with the Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register, which affected supply teachers' pay, do not arise again.
answer_text: This question relates to events in August 2022.A new and improved Northern Ireland Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) was procured by the Education Authority (EA) which administers the system, and it went live in August 2022.A number of issues were identified when the system went live which resulted in the late salary payments for some supply teachers. Improvements have been made in the booking and sign off processes to avoid issues which had created problems in the past and NISTR is accepted as the only system through which schools can engage supply teachers in Northern Ireland. The EA continues to monitor activities through NISTR.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what monitoring is in place to ensure welfare standards are being enforced on a uniform and equal basis by inspectors across all of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Responsibility for enforcing the welfare of animals sits with three enforcement bodies.  My Department is responsible for enforcing standards in relation to farmed animals, local councils enforce standards for non-farmed animals and the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) enforces welfare standards for wild animals. Local councils and the PSNI are accountable for the actions of their respective inspectors.  My Department has no role in monitoring them.The minimum accepted welfare standards for all animals are set out in legislation. Any animal welfare inspections taken forward by the three enforcement bodies must be conducted in accordance with the provisions contained in that legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the consultation on the proposed content of the new Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030.
answer_text: My Department consulted on the Proposed Content of the new Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030 from 15th November 2021 - 10th January 2022.  The consultation analysis was used to inform the content of the Draft Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030 which I published on 24th October 2022. Details of the Consultation Outcome Report and the draft Strategy are available here https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/consultation-outcome-report-new-road-safety-strategy-northern-ireland-2030The draft Strategy provides a high-level Action Plan for 2022/23 outlining measures to be taken forward by key delivery partners in the short term. Publication will allow my officials to continue to develop work on the Strategy, including the identification of Key Performance Indicators and to progress the Actions identified.Due to the ongoing absence of an Executive, which has prevented me from seeking Executive approval for this cross-Departmental Strategy, I secured the support of my Ministerial Colleagues for the publication of this. The draft Strategy will be presented to a future Executive for approval at the earliest opportunity.

answer_text: My Department has spent £6,311.21 on hospitality since February 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what is the projected overspend in his Department; and (ii) what savings have been identified.
answer_text: (i) My Department is facing unprecedented resource pressures in 2022-23, amounting to over £200m, as a result of the increase in energy prices and inflation, which remain extremely volatile. This is assessed against the resource planning envelopes provided by the Department of Finance in the absence of an Executive agreed budget. My officials are continuing to assess the energy price cap announced by the UK Government which may impact this position. In relation to capital my Department continues to maximise capital expenditure in line with the capital allocations I agreed for 2022-23 in the absence of an Executive agreed budget.(ii) The vast majority of my Department’s resource budget is directed at front-line service provision, with most of this being regulated, statutory, fixed or contractually obliged. As a result there is little opportunity for my Department to make immediate savings, without impacting directly on the essential public services it provides and on the health and safety of citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what directions or requirements have been placed on district councils as a result of the EU Protocol.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the Highway Code advocates that cyclists should use cycle lanes where provided, (i) what steps are being taken to encourage this; and (ii) what consideration is being given to making failure to use the cycle lane, without reasonable excuse, a road traffic offence.
answer_text: The Highway Code is the official guide to using our roads safely and legally and it is the responsibility for every road user to be aware of its contents for the safety of themselves and other road users.  Many of the rules of the Highway Code are legal requirements and if you disobey these rules you are committing a criminal offence.  Such rules will contain the words MUST/MUST NOT and also state the effective legislation.  Rules 59-85 in the Highway Code pertain to cyclists.  The Department’s safe travel social media channels on FacebookShareTheRoadToZero and TwitterRoadToZero regularly deliver messages raising awareness of the Highway Code NI Rule 63 which provides advice on the use of cycle lanes.At present no consideration is being given to making failure to use the cycle lane, without reasonable excuse, a road traffic offence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what will be the impact on Northern Ireland as a result of the EU Industrial Emission Directive and the associated costs.
answer_text: The EU Industrial Emission Directive has been brought into effect in Northern Ireland by the Pollution Prevention and Control Regulations (NI) 2013, as amended.The EU is currently carrying out a publicised revision of the EU Directive 2010/75/EU, often known as the Industrial Emissions Directive (“IED”). The IED has been in operation for several years, so it is assumed that the Member’s question relates to the EU’s proposed revisions.There are some new provisions in the EU’s revision of the IED which, if brought into finalised law, may impact upon NI. However, these proposals are still at the preliminary stage.In addition, the only provisions that may currently apply to NI would be those that specifically relate to Large Combustion Plants (“LCPs”) feeding into the single energy market. This is because, of all the provisions of the IED, the NI Protocol only includes LCPs that contribute to the single energy market. For all the other areas applying to the IED, NI is, at least currently, aligned with GB. For these reasons, it is difficult to ascertain at this early stage what the impact and the associated costs will be for NI of the proposed revision to the IED.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the current policy permits the removal and non-replacement of street lighting, that has been in place for decades, without either notification or consultation with affected residents.
answer_text: When street lighting is deemed to have reached the end of its life and needs to be replaced, the area is assessed and new lighting is provided only in those areas that meet my Department’s policy for the provision of lighting. In areas, such as pathways which only provide access to the rear of properties, or roads that are not adopted by my Department, that do not meet this criteria, the existing lighting will be removed but new lighting is not provided. In these circumstances the lighting is being removed for safety reasons and notification and consultation in such circumstances is not required.While I can appreciate the concerns of residents who benefitted from the lighting previously provided, this policy helps to ensure that my Department’s street lighting budget is directed at providing lighting in those areas where maximum benefit to the public can be derived, particularly where road safety is concerned, as well as reducing future maintenance and energy costs and environmental impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment, particularly in respect of Tuberculosis restrictions, of EU Animal Welfare laws being applicable in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As you are aware, due to the Northern Protocol, a range of EU legislation relating to both animal disease control and animal welfare remains applicable in Northern Ireland.		On bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in particular, the Northern Ireland bTB programme, along with bTB programmes throughout the UK, has been aligned with European legislative requirements for several decades.  Our disease control measures are essential to facilitate trade in cattle and cattle products, worth over £1.5 billion each year to our economy.		Officials continue to work on ensuring our bTB programme complies with international standards of animal health.  This will assist our beef and dairy 	industries to have continued access to markets in Europe, while also helping to assure other global trading partners of the high standards of animal health in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission how many MLAs responded to its survey indicating they intend to make substantive contributions in plenary sessions and in committee sittings in (i) Irish; and (ii) Ulster Scots.
answer_text: Following the May 2022 election all Members were surveyed to assess the level of demand for interpretation of Assembly business in Irish and Ulster Scots. 30 Members responded to the survey:10 Members indicated that they intend to make substantive contributions in Irish during Assembly business (plenary and committee).No Member indicated that they intend to make substantive contributions in Ulster Scots during Assembly business.20 Members indicated that they did not intend to make substantive contributions in either Irish or Ulster Scots during Assembly business.As no Members have indicated that they intend to make contributions in Ulster Scots, no evidence has been provided to demonstrate that there is a demand from Members for the interpretation of Assembly business in Ulster Scots. Therefore, on the basis of the evidence provided, the Assembly Commission will provide simultaneous and passive interpretation of Assembly business in Irish only at this point.Should there be a change in circumstances and any Member indicates in advance that they intend to make a contribution during Assembly business in Ulster Scots, this will be accommodated subject to the availability of interpreters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the Protocol, what are the risks to the local farming industry of the impact of Plant Protection Products restrictions in terms of (i) supply issues; (ii) GB companies discontinuing trade; and (iii) prohibition on using products available in the rest of the UK.
answer_text: Departmental officials regularly liaise with manufacturers and grower groups on issues around Plant Protection Product availability arising from the Northern Ireland Protocol and the longer-term impacts of the UK’s exit from the EU.	Growers have indicated that they are having problems accessing some Plant Protection Products as a result of the Protocol.  However, information received from manufacturers and local distributors suggests that whilst it may take longer to move goods into NI from GB, due to the paperwork needed (supplementary declarations, customs etc), supplies of authorised Plant Protection Products to NI currently remain largely unaffected by the Protocol to date. Recent indications are that the supply route to NI has changed in response to the Protocol, with EU and Internationally based manufacturers exporting through Ireland, rather than through GB. 		I am not aware of any specific cases of GB companies ceasing trade in Plant Protection Products to NI, where the products are approved for use in NI.  Pesticide authorisation holders are generally multi-national agrochemical companies with manufacturing largely outside of GB. Nevertheless, some pesticide manufacturers have suggested that the NI market may prove to be too small to cover the costs of future investment in an application for authorisation for NI. Therefore, as products come up for renewal or are being brought to the market for the first time, some companies may make a commercial decision not to submit an application for use in NI.  I am aware that farmers are justifiably concerned that this will limit access to some Plant Protection Products, especially for smaller sectors.	Where there is no divergence with EU conditions of authorisations or EU Maximum Residue Limits (MRL’s), a GB and NI authorisation may be issued enabling NI growers access to the same products as GB growers. However, the timing of EU and GB decisions, and different decisions being taken, both as part of active substance reviews and MRL changes, means that divergence is occuring. This has resulted in the unavailability of some GB authorised pesticides in NI, which may be perceived by NI growers as giving GB growers a commercial advantage. The impact of divergence to date has been minimal or likely to be short term.  However, changes to the GB regulatory regime, future trade deals and other factors may increase divergence and risk to access to products. The Department continues to monitor the situation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what is the risk to the ware potato production arising from the Protocol; and (ii) whether there is an adverse impact on the importation of GB seed potatoes.
answer_text: The main risk to Northern Ireland ware potato production, flows from the dependence of this sector on sourcing seed potatoes, of particular varieties, from GB, to meet established local market demand for ware potatoes. The supply of seed potato varieties used by ware potato growers have traditionally been sourced from Scotland. Due to prohibitions on seed potatoes, as a result of the implementation of the Protocol, this supply is now unavailable. I have repeatedly raised the unacceptability of this situation with HMG.I am aware, that the sector recognises, that seed potato production within Northern Ireland is currently insufficient to enable local growers to meet ware market requirements. My Department has confirmed that it will assist the sector, where possible, in its consideration of improving the sustainability and resilience of the sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what percentage of currently available veterinary medicines will be adversely affected from 31 December 2022 by the application of EU Laws; and (ii) what is the anticipated impact on the farming industry.
answer_text: Under the Northern Ireland (NI) Protocol, veterinary medicines moving from Great Britain to NI are to be subject to additional import requirements. Data from industry, collated by the UK Veterinary Medicines Directorate, suggests that 51% of veterinary medicines would be discontinued in NI if Protocol requirements are applied.	This includes some critical products such as cardiovascular medicines, anaesthetics and vaccines, including those that prevent salmonella and E. coli.  The discontinuation of vaccines in NI would have particularly severe repercussions for animal health and welfare here and give rise to unnecessary animal suffering not to mention the risk it would present to human health and trade.  This could, in turn, impact on the wider agricultural industry as animal health is directly related to the productivity and profitability of farm businesses.	I have made my concerns regarding the threat the Protocol poses to the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland clear to the UK Government, which leads on the relevant EU negotiations. I have also written directly to the European Commission calling for an urgent resolution to the matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the Chief Veterinary Officer’s assessment of (i) the impact of the end of the Protocol grace period in December 2022 on veterinary medicines; and (ii) whether the impact is a commercial matter or one that will have a direct impact on the agricultural industry arising from the Protocol.
answer_text: Under the Northern Ireland Protocol, veterinary medicines moving from Great Britain to Northern Ireland are to be subject to additional import requirements.  The Chief Veterinary Officer for Northern Ireland is satisfied that, if a solution to address these requirements is not found or implemented, a significant number of important medicines for the prevention and treatment of animal disease will either not be available in Northern Ireland, or be significantly more expensive, at the end of the grace period.		The additional import requirements arising would, therefore, have a commercial impact as they would impose an extra, and potentially insurmountable, burden on pharmaceutical companies, wholesale dealers and retailers that move veterinary medicines into Northern Ireland. 		The discontinuation of certain medications from the Northern Ireland market would impact significantly on animal health and welfare here.  This could, in turn, impact on the wider agricultural industry as animal health is directly related to the productivity and profitability of farm businesses.	I have made my concerns regarding the threat the Protocol poses to the supply of veterinary medicines to Northern Ireland clear to the UK Government, which leads on the relevant EU negotiations. I have also written directly to the European Commission calling for an urgent resolution to the matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why planning applications for Certificates of Lawful Development are not hosted on the Planning Portal; and (ii) whether he will review this policy.
answer_text: Requests for Certificates of Lawful Use or Development (CLUD) are not applications for planning permission.  They are requests for formal confirmation on whether planning permission is required for a proposed or existing use or development and therefore whether a planning application needs to be submitted or not. As such, they are not subject to the same process as a formal planning application.  They are   not advertised in local press and neighbour notification letters are not issued.  Consequently, they are not published online as public comments are not sought as part of the process. However, the Councils are required to maintain a register of certificates issued and to make these available to the public. Therefore a member of the public can ask the Council whether any certificates exist for a particular property and request to view or purchase a copy of these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why the NI Substitute Teacher Register system for paying teachers has been failing; and (ii) what assurances can be given that all substitute teachers will be paid on time this month.
answer_text: The core purpose of the NI Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) is to provide a booking system which enables all schools to source, contact and book qualified, pre-employment checked teachers with the relevant expertise to meet the needs of the cover required.  The Department funds the Education Authority (EA) to contract a supplier to provide the system and to provide system administration on behalf of all grant-aided schools.  The NISTR system does not pay teachers however a file containing all bookings approved by schools before the payroll deadline is transferred to the Department for processing of salary where relevant.Whilst the majority of teachers who worked in August were paid via the temporary payroll on the 12th working day of the month, it is entirely regrettable that not all teachers received salary for all or some of the days worked for various reasons not all of which were linked to the new NISTR system.  The delay in go-live until 29 August and technical issues experienced in the first few days necessitated the manual input of thousands of retrospective bookings by the NISTR Support Team on schools’ behalf.  Several hundred bookings were not confirmed by teachers or approved by schools before the payroll deadline.  A small number of users could not confirm or sign-off due to access issues.  A substantial number of teachers were already working on contracts paid through the permanent payroll and therefore payment for NISTR days was included in their pay check at the end of the month.  Numerous teachers had not yet provided bank details to payroll and there were a number of coding and duplication errors within the transferred file preventing payment for all bookings.A number of fixes have been applied to provide additional functionality and address identified bugs and the new system is now functioning adequately.  Work continues to make system improvements based on user feedback and additional features will continue to be added throughout the development phase.The Department and the NISTR Support Team are working intensively to ensure that salary payment for all approved bookings for September days (and any outstanding days from August) will be paid through the relevant payroll this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether classroom assistants in any sector receive pay over school holidays; and (ii) whether she has plans to introduce payment to classroom assistants across schools during school holidays.
answer_text: The majority of Classroom Assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland are employed by the Education Authority (EA) on Conditions of Service determined by the National Joint Council (NJC) for Local Government Services. Within these Conditions there are various arrangements in place that impact on how Classroom Assistants are paid and whether they are required to work and receive payment over school holidays. The three main types of Classroom Assistant contracts are as follows:  Term time working - in these cases, staff are paid for hours worked, with a further payment in lieu of annual leave. 52 week contracts - these require classroom assistants to be on duty for contracted weekly hours during periods of school closure except when on annual leave or other authorised absence. 52 weeks contracts, with an option to request periods of unpaid leave during extended school holidays or closures.In line with established negotiating arrangements, Trade Unions have recently raised with EA Management the issue of existing term time staff moving to a 52 week contract, if they wish to do so.  This would enable these staff to continue working and receive payment over the school holidays.The EA are currently considering this request and will be liaising further with Trade Unions on this issue.  They note that it may be appropriate to consider this change within the wider context of a non-teaching pay & grading review and the SEND Transformation Programme, a strand of which includes a project to review the Special Classroom Assistant Employment model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department’s expenditure on photography since January 2020; and to outline the rationale for this expenditure.
answer_text: Photography is an essential part of Government and Ministerial communications. My Department has a diverse and portfolio with responsibility for food, farming, environmental, fisheries, forestry and sustainable policy and the development of the rural sector across the whole of Northern Ireland. Each of these sectors has been impacted greatly by the pandemic, war in Ukraine and rising cost of living, food, supplies and fuel as well as the ongoing avian flu season. Following Covid-19, DAERA had a considerable backlog of engagements with a large number of stakeholders on a wide range of issues affecting their sectors. In my role, it is often necessary to visit rural locations throughout Northern Ireland. This means that photographers are required for extended periods of time. It is also only to be expected that these costs will be higher in the years when Ministers were in office.  Due to the wide-ranging scope of my Department, photographers are also required for a number of non-Ministerial events across all areas of DAERA. This includes promotion of education, knowledge transfer and innovation programmes at   the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) and work of the Northern Ireland Environment Agency. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what steps can be taken to avoid caravan owners being charged commercial rates on their caravans.
answer_text: The Rates (Amendment) Order (Northern Ireland) 2006 made a provision that from 1 April 2007 leisure van pitches in caravan parks were no longer to be treated as domestic and would thereafter be valued for rates as non-domestic property.  Primary legislation would be required to change this position and that is not possible in the absence of a functioning Executive and Assembly.The provision of Covid-19 rate support for 27 months from April 2020 to the end of June 2022 to privately run caravan parks was made on the condition that the support was passed on by the owner to the occupiers of the site.  In the continuing absence of a functioning Executive, it is not possible to allocate the resources that would be required to extend the rates holiday.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what specific assistance currently exists for small companies in the hospitality sector that are struggling with energy costs.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressure that businesses are facing across the board, primarily as a result of soaring wholesale energy costs associated with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. In the last year, since September 2021, the wholesale cost of natural gas has trebled (+194%), while the cost of coal (+104%) and home heating oil (+107%) has doubled. Coupled with ongoing global supply chain disruptions, these unprecedented increases in energy costs are driving inflation across the economy, including input cost pressures for small businesses in hospitality and other sectors. As we are seeing across Europe and beyond, the magnitude of these global inflationary pressures means that it can only be tackled meaningfully by significant interventions at a national government level. That is why in recent weeks and months I have taken every opportunity to engage with UK Government to highlight the inflationary pressures being felt by households and businesses in Northern Ireland. Following these engagements, I welcome the UK Government’s commitment to deliver the much needed £400 Energy Support Payment Scheme in Northern Ireland. In addition, the announcement by the Prime Minister of a domestic Energy Price Guarantee and a non-domestic Energy Price Bill Scheme – her commitment that the same level of support will be made available in Northern Ireland via a parallel scheme. This relief will give households and businesses a degree of certainty with their bills over the coming months.  My Department will continue to engage with the UK Government and others to ensure these support measures reach Northern Ireland households and businesses as soon as possible.The outlook remains challenging but I am hopeful for the future. I am investing in those areas where we can gain the best long-term outcomes, which will help us build greater economic resilience for the future.  For example, I have published an energy strategy which will deliver a step change in how we move away from our reliance on overseas, imported fossil fuels. Alongside our investment in skills this will support the delivery of our ambitious 10X Economic Vision for the benefit of all our people.In practical terms, Invest Northern Ireland’s Energy and Resource Efficiency Support for businesses provides a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable local businesses to become more efficient and resilient through green efficiencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how far the Regional Dementia Care Pathway, announced in 2018, has been fully funded or implemented.
answer_text: The Integrated Care Partnerships, who will be responsible for implementation of the pathway, have submitted business cases in line with the commissioner’s service specifications. The business cases outline the additional resource requirements to implement the pathway in:An identified prototype site in their respective Trust localities. All areas of Northern Ireland. It is acknowledged that implementation of the pathway in either of these two options will require substantial financial investment in the region of millions of pounds.Implementation of the regional dementia care pathway is required to address the current presenting challenges with delivering effective, safe and high quality dementia services. The regional dementia care pathway outlines a transformational service delivery model which will require a primary care led approach working closely with secondary care dementia services. At the current time there is no identified source of funding to implement the regional dementia care pathway. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what commitment she can give to retaining the statutory provisions protecting religious education in Articles 21 and 22 of The Education and Libraries (NI) Order 1986.
answer_text: I support the right of the many thousands of children of all denominations to have an education which is based on Christian ethos and values.  This includes the provision of Religious Education and collective worship in our schools.  I will not repeal the legislation underpinning the Christian values and teaching in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why goods being imported to Northern Ireland from China and other countries are being charged a higher third country duty tariff than goods imported from those countries to GB.
answer_text: Under the current terms of the Protocol, traders importing goods which originate from countries outside of both the European Union (EU) and United Kingdom (UK) to Northern Ireland (NI) are required to pay either the EU or UK duty. 	Traders will be unable to pay the UK duty, and therefore subject to the EU duty, where the trader is unable to verify that the goods are ‘not at risk’ of onward movement to the EU. The EU duty will also automatically apply where it is higher than the applicable UK duty and the difference is equal to or greater than 3 percentage points, or if the goods are subject to any EU trade remedies.	Where NI businesses are subject to a higher EU imposed duty, these businesses may be placed at a disadvantage in comparison to competitors in GB. I have raised this with the UK Government as it is precisely this sort of problem that has highlighted issues with the operation of the Protocol in respect of a functioning UK internal market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many abortions have been performed since the Abortion (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2020 commenced on 31 March 2020 broken down by Health Trust.
answer_text: The Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No.2) Regulations 2020 came into force on 31 March 2020, legalising abortion in Northern Ireland.  The Regulations require a notice of each termination to be sent to the Chief Medical Officer.  The number of notifications received to date (up to 26 September 2022), broken down by each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust, are as follows:Belfast HSC Trust (1313), Western HSC Trust (241), Southern HSC Trust (703), South Eastern HSC Trust (882) and Northern HSC Trust (997), creating a total 4136.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will regularly publish the abortion figures broken down by Health Trust, including the reason for each abortion.
answer_text: Statistical information on terminations of pregnancy based on hospital admissions is published annually by my Department and can be found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/termination-pregnancy.The lawful grounds for termination of pregnancy are set out in Regulations 3, 4, 5, 6 and 7 of the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No 2) Regulations 2020. In line with Regulation 10 I do not intend to publish any information relating to the grounds for each termination.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many abortions were performed in the three years prior to the commencement of the Abortion (Northern Ireland) Regulations 2020.
answer_text: I refer to my responses to AQW 7724/17-22 and AQW 3745/17-22.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the reasons for abortions in each of the last five years, broken down by Health Trust.
answer_text: My response to AQW 3492/22-27 provides the number of abortion notifications received by my Department since 31 March 2020 under the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No 2) Regulations 2020 (“the 2020 Regulations”).  These notifications have been tallied and broken down by Trust but not otherwise processed.  As Regulation 10 of the 2020 Regulations restricts the disclosure of the information contained in the notifications, I am unable to provide a breakdown of the grounds for these procedures.  The lawful grounds for termination of pregnancy are set out in Regulations 3, 4, 5, 6 and 7 of the 2020 Regulations.Abortion statistics for the years prior to 2020 can been found on my Department’s website at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-termination-pregnancy-statistics-201213-201920.  However these publications do not provide detail regarding the grounds for termination.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much has been spent on providing abortion services in each Health and Social Care Trust since 1 March 2020.
answer_text: The Department of Health has not commissioned abortion services and has therefore not provided any funding to the Trusts to provide abortion services.  While the Trusts are providing limited abortion services, they are doing so within their own resources and in accordance with the Abortion (Northern Ireland) (No.2) Regulations 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what assessment has been made of the impact of the absence of a hard shoulder on the A5.
answer_text: The new A5 Western Transport Corridor dual carriageway has been designed in accordance with modern day standards as set out in the Design Manual for Roads and Bridges. These standards dictate that hard shoulders are now only provided on motorway roads, with hard strips being provided on all dual carriageway roads.These hard strips serve a number of purposes including helping to provide pavement integrity/stability; partial provision for stopped vehicles; snow and water collection; an overrun facility for driver error or evasive action; and to allow for the placement of road studs (cat eyes) outside vehicle wheel paths.The proposed layout for the A5 is a significant improvement over the current arrangements and recent schemes that have incorporated this road layout are: A6 Randalstown to Castledawson; A4 Dungannon to Ballygawley; and A8 Belfast to Larne.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration has been given to the equality implications of refusing patients access to pastoral care from a minister of their choice.
answer_text: All Health and Social Care Trust Hospital Chaplaincies provides religious, pastoral and spiritual care to all persons of faith and none.  All Trusts have chaplains representing the main denominations in the Trust catchment area i.e. Church of Ireland, Methodist, Presbyterian and Roman Catholic Denominations.  Where the Chaplaincy team does not include a member of a specific faith group trusts have nominated members of the team who with the permission of the patient will contact the minister/religious leader of their choice and arrange for them to attend the hospital. The Southern Trust has contact details for the Jehovah’s Witness Centre, the Islamic Centre and a Jewish minister of religion.  All HSC Trusts recognise the valuable support that patients/families can receive from their Minister of religion or Faith/Belief representative, therefore I do not believe that any minister of religion has been refused access to attend to a patient’s pastoral care.  On this basis the Department does not have any equality concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what is the projected cost and timescale of the current work to provide cycle paths on the Coleraine ring road.
answer_text: The Coleraine Ring Road Cycle path scheme, which it is estimated will cost approximately £1.9M, began on 25 July 2022 and completion is anticipated at the end of January 2023.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many requests to access a minister of religion from a denomination other than the Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church of Ireland or Methodist churches have been declined in each of Northern Ireland's hospitals since March 2020.
answer_text: The information requested is not held centrally within my Department so each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) were asked for input. Western, Belfast, South Eastern and Southern HSCT were able to reply within the timeframe given. Western HSCT have stated that although the figures are not collated, if a patient requests spiritual support from any denomination, the visit is facilitated in line with normal visiting, and any COVID restrictions. The Risk Management and Complaints Departments within the trust have confirmed that there have been no issues raised or complaints received relating to such matters. Belfast HSCT also stated that their Chaplaincy Department do not collate these figures, nor would it be practical for wards to do so. During the Covid-19 pandemic, visiting has been in line with the Department’s visiting guidance and any necessary risk assessments. The Trust does not refuse patients access to pastoral care from a minister of their choice as it recognises the valuable support that patients and families can receive from their minister of religion, faith or belief. Their Spiritual Care policy (2019) states: “Spiritual care should be sensitive to the rights of individuals to determine their own needs in regard to spiritual care, including their right to be visited (or not visited) by any chaplain, member of the clergy, or faith/community/belief group representative”.This is the general practice, however there may be times where this is assessed as not possible for any external visits e.g. Covid outbreaks, or patient safety. Hospital chaplaincy representatives who are fit-tested will continue to offer visiting during a Covid-19 outbreak. Where in-person visits of the minister of their choice are not possible, virtual visiting using iPads is facilitated. Where it is safe to do so, every effort is made to accommodate visits by ministers of religion or faith/belief representatives, especially in end-of-life situations.Southern Trust confirmed that they do not believe that any minister of religion from any denomination have been declined a visit in a hospital since March 2020, however this information is not recorded.
