question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the extent to which SONI Ltd helped shape his Department’s Energy Strategy; and (ii) to outline how the Energy Strategy ensures that electricity transmission and the planning thereof remains locally controlled.
answer_text: (i), SONI participated in the Power Theme Energy Strategy Working Group, alongside other key organisations and industry stakeholders, which contributed to the development of the Energy Strategy.  SONI also provided responses to the Department’s consultations on the Energy Strategy, which are publicly available on our Departmental website.(ii) The overarching mission of the Northern Ireland Energy Strategy is to achieve self-sufficiency in affordable renewable energy.  To deliver the strategy, we must ensure the electricity transmission system can accommodate the forthcoming demand from, for example, the electrification of heat and transport.  SONI is obliged to develop a ten-year programme of work known as the “Transmission Development Plan for Northern Ireland”.  The Utility Regulator requires SONI to update and consult on this plan annually under Condition 40 of its Transmission System Operator licence. The plan includes, among other things, investment needs and planned network development.  This requirement is set out in The Electricity (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 under Section 11A.  SONI is obliged to adhere to this condition in order to comply with requirements in the Electricity Directive.  Please see this link for the most recent plan published on the SONI website:   https://www.soni.ltd.uk/the-grid/projects/tdpni/what-is-happening-now/

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of whether the governance arrangements of SONI have had an impact on the prices Northern Ireland consumers pay for electricity.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator (UR) is an independent body responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland and its work on the review of the System Operator for Northern Ireland’s (SONI) governance structures is in line with its statutory duties.		My Department is not in a position to provide an assessment of whether the governance arrangements of SONI have had an impact on prices Northern Ireland consumers pay for electricity, that is being addressed by the UR through its work on SONI governance. 		The UR has, however, advised me that the current SONI governance arrangements could potentially give rise to inappropriately higher prices for NI consumers. Consequently, it is important that SONI is accountable to the UR and NI stakeholders, and that its decisions are transparent. The UR’s licence modification will provide greater transparency, including of cost information, together with an effective SONI Board to oversee and approve the SONI’s costs.		I continue to support the work of the UR in promoting and protecting the interests of electricity consumers. It is essential that appropriate governance arrangements, consistent with legislative requirements and underpinned by licence conditions are in place for SONI, ensuring that the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are paramount. The UR has taken steps to address concerns, including those in relation to cost, through the proposed licence modifications

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what is the budget of Northern Division for (i) active travel projects, including cycle paths; and (ii) road maintenance, including repair of potholes.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive, budgets have not been agreed for the 2022-23 financial year. However, to ensure that capital spending is maximised to deliver much needed investment in our infrastructure, I agreed capital allocations for my Department, which include the following for Northern Division. (i) I allocated £3.3m capital for Blue/Green active travel projects which will benefit Northern Division. This includes Pedestrian and Cycling Measures. Additionally there may be some active travel expenditure planned as part of network development and Strategic Road Improvement projects, however, it is not possible to disaggregate that investment from the relevant scheme allocation.(ii) For road maintenance, I have allocated an opening amount of £15.4m capital to Northern Division to permit delivery of essential structural maintenance.  Resource allocations for road maintenance activities, including the repair of potholes, have not been finalised due to the significant inescapable resource pressures facing my Department this financial year and possible actions that may be required to reduce expenditure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to the Utility Regulator’s SONI TSO Governance report published on 30 August 2022, (i) whether Northern Ireland electricity consumers are paying higher prices as a result of the current governance arrangements; and (ii) what action he intends to take to mitigate the potential for inappropriately higher prices for Northern Ireland consumers.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator (UR) is an independent body responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland and its work on the review of the System Operator for Northern Ireland’s (SONI) governance structures is in line with its statutory duties.The UR has advised me that the current SONI governance arrangements could potentially give rise to inappropriately higher prices for NI consumers.  Consequently, it is important that SONI is accountable to the UR and NI stakeholders, and that its decisions are transparent. The UR’s licence modification will provide greater transparency, including of cost information, together with an effective SONI Board to oversee and approve SONI’s costs.		I continue to support the work of the UR in promoting and protecting the interests of electricity consumers. It is essential that appropriate governance arrangements, consistent with legislative requirements and underpinned by licence conditions are in place for SONI, ensuring that the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are paramount. The UR has taken steps to address concerns, including those in relation to cost, through the proposed licence modifications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the findings of the Utility Regulator’s SONI TSO Governance report, published on 30 August 2022, that the management and oversight of SONI TSO licence responsibilities are effectively discharged by EirGrid plc and not by SONI Ltd.
answer_text: Whilst the Utility Regulator (UR) is an independent body, responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland, I greatly value the work carried out in regards to modifying the conditions of SONI’s Transmission System Operator’s licence. 		It is vitally important that the interests of the Northern Ireland consumer are protected. The modifications are designed to mitigate risks identified by the UR, enhance the independence of SONI, improve the transparency and accountability of the company through better governance and management, and ultimately ensure the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are protected.		I continue to support the work of the UR in promoting and protecting the interests of electricity consumers and I consider it essential that appropriate governance arrangements, consistent with legislative requirements and underpinned by licence conditions, are in place for SONI, ensuring that the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are paramount. The UR has taken steps to address the concerns highlighted in its report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the powers of the Utility Regulator in overseeing and requiring change in relation to SONI.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator (UR) is the independent body responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland. The Electricity (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 provides it with the power to licence SONI and the power to make modifications to that licence following the process set out within that legislation. 		I greatly value the work of the UR in regards to modifying the conditions of SONI’s Transmission System Operator’s licence. It is vitally important that the interests of the Northern Ireland consumer are protected. The modifications are designed to mitigate risks identified by the UR, enhance the independence of SONI, improve the transparency and accountability of the company through better governance and management, and ultimately ensure the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are protected.		I continue to support the work of the UR in promoting and protecting the interests of electricity consumers and I consider it essential that appropriate governance arrangements, consistent with legislative requirements and underpinned by licence conditions, are in place for SONI, ensuring that the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are paramount. The UR has taken steps to address the concerns highlighted in its report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has the power to change the terms of the licence of the System Operator for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action he is taking to address the findings of the Utility Regulator’s report, SONI TSO Governance, in relation to the independence of SONI and a potential breach of its licence.
answer_text: Whilst the Utility Regulator (UR) is an independent body, responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland, I greatly value the work carried out in regards to modifying the conditions of SONI's transmission system operator's license. It is vitally important that the interests of the Northern Ireland consumer are protected. The modifications are designed to mitigate risks identified by the UR, enhance the independence of SONI, improve the transparency and accountability of the company through better governance and management, and ultimately ensure the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are protected.It is essential that its governance and management structures command public confidence and are fit for purpose in dealing with the challenges ahead.I continue to support the work of the UR in promoting and protecting the interests of electricity consumers and I consider it essential that appropriate governance arrangements, consistent with legislative requirements and underpinned by licence conditions, are in place for SONI, ensuring that the interests of Northern Ireland consumers are paramount. The UR has taken steps to address the concerns highlighted in its report.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the repairs needed to the roof of Parliament Buildings; and whether legal proceedings have been issued in relation to these repairs.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has obtained expert advice on the necessary repairs to the roof of Parliament Buildings. Any urgent holding repairs identified by those experts have been carried out.In respect of issues related to the roof, the Assembly Commission has issued proceedings against Hamilton Architects LLP and Tracey Brothers Ltd for negligence, breach of statutory duty and breach of contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps have been taken to require a review by the Education Authority of its Guidance for Schools, EOTAS Centres and Youth Service on Supporting Transgender Young People, following publication of the Interim Report of the Cass Review.
answer_text: It is my intention to review the guidance and I have asked my officials to draft the Terms of Reference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many referrals there have been by Knowing Our Identity, Knockbracken, or other services, to the Tavistock and Portman Gender Identity Disorder Service (i) before; and (ii) after publication of the Interim Report of the Cass Review.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has confirmed that the Adult Gender Clinic and the Knowing Our Identity service have not made any referrals to the Tavistock and Portman Gender Identity Disorder Service before or after the publication of the Interim Report of the Cass Review.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) to detail its policy for granting retirement to Secretariat staff and then re-engaging them on a part time basis; (ii) to detail the Assembly posts currently filled through this process; and (iii) to detail the rationale for this position.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission does not have a policy for granting retirement to its staff and then re-engaging them on a part-time basis and there are currently no Assembly Commission posts filled in this way.
